framework: Ofsted education inspection framework (for use from November 2025) - School inspection toolkit

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders have established an open culture in which safeguarding is everyone’s responsibility. Multi-agency working is effective. There is strategic oversight of all aspects of safeguarding and promoting the welfare of pupils. Leaders actively try to learn from safeguarding cases and incidents and take any action needed.
suggested_evidence: Leaders foster an open culture in which safeguarding is understood as everyone’s responsibility.
Strategic oversight is evident through regular review of safeguarding practice, learning from incidents, and action taken to address any identified weaknesses.
Multi-agency working is effective, with timely referrals and information sharing.
Safeguarding practice is quality assured internally and externally.

judgement_areas.statement: Pupils are kept safe and feel safe. Their voices are heard, including the voices of pupils who are not on the school site (whether long term, temporarily or for part of the school day). Teaching pupils about how they can stay safe and when they may need help is embedded across the curriculum. Pupils and parents know who to go to for support.
suggested_evidence: Pupils report that they feel safe and know who to go to for help.
Safeguarding, including online safety and Prevent, is embedded across the curriculum.
Pupil voice is gathered regularly, including from pupils educated off‑site or in alternative provision, and leaders act on concerns raised.
Parents and carers are informed about safeguarding arrangements and points of contact.
Arrangements for pupils not on site ensure leaders know where pupils are and that absence is followed up promptly.

judgement_areas.statement: All staff are vigilant and carry out their responsibilities effectively to keep pupils safe. Staff understand the signs of possible safeguarding concerns. They respond by following the school’s systems confidently and consistently.
suggested_evidence: All staff receive safeguarding induction and regular training, including updates on statutory guidance, Prevent duty and online safety.
Staff confirm they have read and understood Part 1 of Keeping Children Safe in Education and know how to report concerns.
Systems for reporting concerns, managing allegations and low‑level concerns are clear and applied consistently.
Leaders monitor staff practice through supervision, case sampling and review.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders fulfil their responsibilities in relation to child-on-child violence. This includes (but is not limited to) bullying, physical abuse (including physical assault and harm (or the threat of harm) with a weapon), sexual violence and harassment, and domestic abuse in pupils’ own intimate relationships (teenage relationship abuse).
suggested_evidence: Leaders know and fulfil their responsibilities in relation to child‑on‑child abuse, including bullying, sexual violence and harassment.
Incidents are recorded, investigated and responded to promptly.
Leaders analyse patterns and review the effectiveness of actions taken, ensuring appropriate support for both victims and perpetrators, in line with statutory guidance.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders know and fulfil the statutory requirements for safeguarding. These include managing safer recruitment, reporting, referrals, record-keeping and the ‘Prevent’ duty.
suggested_evidence: Leaders fulfil statutory safeguarding duties, including safer recruitment, referrals, record‑keeping, Prevent duty and online filtering and monitoring.
The Single Central Record is complete and regularly reviewed.
Prevent risk assessment reflects local context.
Online filtering and monitoring systems meet DfE core standards and are reviewed for effectiveness.

judgement_areas.statement: The school has clear and accessible policies and procedures that keep pupils safe. Leaders are receptive to challenge and are reflective about their own practices. This means that the impact of safeguarding policies, systems and processes is kept under continual review.
suggested_evidence: Safeguarding policies and procedures are clear, accessible and reviewed regularly.
Leaders are receptive to challenge from governors, auditors and external agencies, and reflect on safeguarding practice to secure improvement.
The impact of safeguarding systems is kept under continual review.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders ensure that staff’s work is monitored and that they get appropriate supervision and support. The conduct and behaviour of staff are appropriate. Leaders follow local authority procedures in managing safeguarding concerns or allegations about adults.
suggested_evidence: Leaders monitor staff conduct and provide appropriate supervision and support.
Local authority procedures are followed when managing safeguarding concerns or allegations about adults.
Whistleblowing procedures are understood by staff.
Safeguarding assurance is in place for third‑party providers and alternative provision.

judgement_areas.statement: Where appropriate, leaders have resolved any minor safeguarding issues identified during the inspection or are taking steps to resolve them.
suggested_evidence: Any minor safeguarding issues identified through inspection or audit are addressed promptly.
Actions are tracked and reviewed to ensure issues are resolved and do not recur.

statements: Serious and/or widespread failures in safeguarding lead to pupils, or particular groups of pupils, being unsafe.

Leaders and those responsible for governance (we will refer to this group as ‘leaders’ throughout this section for ease of reading) have not taken sufficient action to resolve weaknesses following a failure of safeguarding that meant pupils may not have been kept safe.
Leaders are not open to challenge and/or do not learn from issues or incidents.

If pupils are not on the school site (whether long term, temporarily or for part of the school day), leaders are either not clear where the pupils are or are not able to demonstrate that they have taken steps to safeguard them. This includes pupils absent from education and those attending inappropriate, unregistered or unmonitored alternative provision.

Pupils have little confidence that the school will tackle concerns about safety, including the risk of abuse. This is because leaders have not taken their views seriously and/or have not dealt with relevant concerns, so they feel unsafe.

Leaders do not fulfil their responsibilities in relation to child-on-child violence. This includes (but is not limited to) bullying, physical abuse (including physical assault and harm (or the threat of harm) with a weapon), sexual violence and harassment, and domestic abuse in pupils’ own intimate relationships (teenage relationship abuse). Leaders do not handle allegations of abuse swiftly and appropriately. This is likely to lead to pupils being put at risk of significant harm.
Leaders do not fulfil their responsibilities in relation to safer recruitment, reporting, referrals, record-keeping and the ‘Prevent’ duty.
Leaders do not handle safeguarding
notes: Propose don't need suggested evidence as, by default, if any safeguarding is 'red' on the met standard then safeguarding is not met…

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders understand the school’s context, strengths and areas for development. They have a clear rationale for their improvement priorities and largely take appropriate action to drive improvement across all key stages and areas of the school’s work. If an aspect of the school’s provision falls short of the expected standard, this is dealt with quickly and effectively.
key_questions: Do leaders know the school? Where there have been areas that have needed support, have they provided it? Have they taken effective action to identify areas of concern? Can leaders articulate this (e.g. when they visit classrooms?)
suggested_evidence: Leaders produce and update a concise context profile and priorities briefing sheet (context - including demographics of the pupils and their needs - changes, pressures and successes) at least annually
School Inspection Operating Guide p14
Leaders (at middle and senior leadership) can articulate what they aim to achieve, how well they are doing it and how they know it is working
Inspection Information for State-funded schools p9
A succinct SEF where leaders evaluate strengths and succeses, priorities for improvement and where they sit in terms of the 5 point grading scale which is directly linked to monitoring evidence
School Inspection Operating Guide p14; State-funded school inspection toolkit p7
Leaders know, understand and can evidence how well pupils achieve, belong and thrive
Leaders scrutinise reliable assessment data across all subject areas and phases, so that teaching is precisely targeted to improving outcomes for individuals

A live School Improvement Plan (SIP), reviewed on the Trust's defined cycle, aligned to SEF and identification of the right priorities (context-priorities-rationale-impact), with KPIs, owners, milestones and review cycles
State-funded school inspection toolkit p75,
sources: Context profile and priorities briefing sheet (updated annually); SEF (self-evaluation form) with strengths, successes, and improvement priorities; School Improvement Plan (SIP) aligned to SEF, with KPIs, owners, milestones, and review cycles; Assessment data and pupil tracking reports; Staff and governor meeting minutes; Staff voice surveys; “You said, we did” logs
For expected standard, evidence should demonstrate that systems and processes are in place and are used to inform improvement.

judgement_areas.statement: Governors/trustees ensure that the vision, ethos and strategic direction of the school are clearly defined, take account of context, and make sure that resources, including digital technologies, are used effectively. They typically support and challenge leaders appropriately, giving due regard to leaders’ and staff well-being and workload.
key_questions: Can governors articulate the vision, ethos and strategic direction of the school? Is the relationship between governors and the school appropriately professional and based on evidence, rather than familiarity? Are decisions based on triangulated evidence rather than taken at face value? Are governors able to evidence that they challenge and support (verbal articulation and minutes)? Is there a clearly defined approach to how governance tackles/addresses workload and wellbeing?
suggested_evidence: Governors carry out their statutory duties effectively
State-funded school inspection toolkit p75,
Support and robust challenge recorded in minutes—grounded in how governors assure themselves of impact.
School Inspection Operating Guide p44, State-funded school inspection toolkit p75
Governor assurance methods (link visits, learning walks with leaders, triangulation approach, high-quality feedback) are completed regularly, withwritten reports, and inform continuous improvement
State-funded school inspection toolkit p75
Evidence of governor monitoring of use of AP and part-time timetables
School Inspection Operating Guide p50
A clearly defined strategic direction that fits context, with resources aligned.
Consideration of workload and well‑being obligations by the responsible body such as Well‑being & workload framework (policies, pulse surveys, actions, impact) monitoring
State-funded school inspection toolkit p78
Where applicable, the school week meets the DfE's minimum expectation of 32.5 hours
Promotion of effective use of resources, including digital technologies, to support postiive outcomes for children
sources: Governor meeting minutes and reports; Link visit records and learning walk logs; Triangulation approach documentation; Well-being and workload framework (policies, pulse surveys, actions, impact monitoring); Statutory duty records; Evidence of governor monitoring of AP and part-time timetables
For expected standard, evidence should show governors are fulfilling statutory duties and supporting/challenging leaders appropriately

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders are role models of high expectations and professionalism. Staff have high expectations of what pupils can achieve.
key_questions: Are see senior staff visible? What is staff’s perception of the leadership team - do they have confidence in them? Do leaders provide clarity for staff and are ambitious about the standards they expect while taking staff with them and being mindful of workload and wellbeing. Ultimately, do leaders inspire confidence? Where there are any issues/deficiencies with leadership at any level, is this addressed effectively through either support or challenge?
suggested_evidence: Leadership expectations are high, clearly articulated and evidenced in practice and dialogue (staff , familiess, students and visitors are treated with professionalism, courtesy, empathy and respect)
School Inspection Operating Guide p8; State-funded school inspection toolkit p75
Leaders keep records of annual staff voice surveys evidencing confidence in leadership and clarity of expectations
State-funded school inspection toolkit p75
A visible professional culture and high expectations that translate into consistent practice
Pupils experience a positive and enriching school life
Clear and ambitious vision for providing high-quality education to all pupils which is regularly reviewed to check alignment with practice
sources: Leadership expectations documentation; Annual staff survey results; Behaviour logs and pupil voice records; Professional culture evidence (e.g. visitor feedback, staff and pupil surveys)
For expected standard, evidence should show high expectations are articulated and generally evident in practice.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders take action to ensure that staff and governors feel valued and involved in the strategic direction of the school.
key_questions: How do leaders ensure governors and staff are engaged and aligned with the school's strategic direction?
suggested_evidence: Steps are taken for staff and governors to be included, valued and involved
State-funded school inspection toolkit p78
Recruitment and retention is strong and staff absence is low (or improving over time)
There is evidence of the Impact of engagement (staff voice, you said/we did)
sources: Staff and governor engagement logs; Recruitment and retention records; Staff voice surveys and attendance data; “You said, we did” logs
For expected standard, evidence should show steps are taken for staff and governors to be included, valued and involved

judgement_areas.statement: The professional learning and expertise programme is evidence-informed, of high quality and designed to build expertise. It draws on evidence and includes planned opportunities to apply and embed practice to build an effective team of teachers and staff, including ECTs and trainees, where relevant.
key_questions: How is training and CPD actually helping staff to develop and how do you know? Do leaders know where the strengths in teaching and leadesrhip are? Do they know where more intensive support is needed? And, where this has been needed has support been targeted well to make a difference to pupils or has underperformance been left unchallenged? What opportunities do staff have to grow, work together and develop their practice?
suggested_evidence: A coherent PLD curriculum aligned to context and school needs—evidence of rationale and grounding in high‑quality research.
State-funded school inspection toolkit p76
Knowledge of all ECT and/or any trainees on placement. Records show appropriate mentor allocation, QA of mentoring, protected mentor time, ECF training
School Inspection Operating Guide p46; State-funded school inspection toolkit p77
Leaders capture evidence of reviewing impact of professional learning programme that demonstrates it building an effective team of teachers and positive impact on student achievement
State-funded school inspection toolkit p79
Leaders review 1265 annually and carefully consider protected PL time as part of this
Protected time/resources and opportunities to apply and embed practice; purposeful collaboration; effective feedback.
Central register of non-specialist teachers with targeted development for non‑specialist staff which is planned, documented and reviewed for impact.
sources: PLD curriculum documentation; CPD logs and impact evaluations; Mentor allocation records and QA of mentoring; Protected PL time records; Central register of non-specialist teachers and targeted development plans
For expected standard, evidence should show a coherent PLD curriculum aligned to context and school needs

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders support staff’s well-being and ensure that their workload is manageable. Leaders have systems to protect staff from bullying, unlawful discrimination, harassment and victimisation.
key_questions: How do you gather this information? Are there clear systems which formalise this? How do staff feel about these areas? How do you know?
suggested_evidence: Structured systems are in place so that leaders are able to be informed of staff wellbeing and workload
State-funded school inspection toolkit p76
Systems to support staff well-being are published and reviewed e.g. Wellbeing policy/charter, confidential reporting routes
Systems and structures are utilised to provide feedback on workload and wellbeing and impact of actions taken. E.g. workload review cycle, staff surveys, review of staff attendance data, focus group
Staff feel valued as demonstrated through staff voice and attendance.

Staff are aware of school/Trust policies around bullying, unlawful discrimination, harassment and victimisation and understand the systems in place to protect them. Leaders log reported concerns and responses.
sources: Well-being policy/charter; Shared reporting routes; Workload review cycle documentation; Staff surveys and attendance data; Focus group records;
For expected standard, evidence should show structured systems are in place to monitor and support staff well-being and workload

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders and governors develop constructive relationships with all parents and with the wider community, to build trust. They draw on these relationships to support pupils to achieve and feel that they belong.
key_questions: What has the school done to constructively engage parents and involve them as part of the school community? What actions have leaders taken to support and address parents who may be challenging or particularly needy? What relationships has the school developed with the wider community - e.g charity work -involving the community with the school or participation in events?
suggested_evidence: The school logs parent and community engagement activities and strategically plans thoughtful, targeted engagement that improves outcomes/experiences.
State-funded school inspection toolkit p77
Clear log of formal parental complaints that demonstates how the school has responded constructively to concerns raised.
Leaders can articulate details of (and responses to) any recent tensions in, or pressures from, the local community
School Inspection Operating Guide p14
School leaders and govenors monitor parental engagement and maintain reports on how feedback has informed decisions
sources: Parent and community engagement logs; Partnership network participation records; Formal parental complaints log and response records; Reports on how feedback has informed decisions
For expected standard, evidence should show constructive engagement and response to feedback.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders work with other schools, organisations and professionals in a culture of mutual support and challenge.
key_questions: Does the school adtively engage with external partners and networks to support improvement?
suggested_evidence: Leaders maintain an up-to-date list of external professionals and services involved with pupils, with logs of referrals, meetings and outcomes
School Inspection Operating Guide p15
Records show participation in partnership networks or school-school collaborations and the resulting impact on provision.
State-funded school inspection toolkit p77
sources: External professional and service involvement logs; Partnership network participation records; Referral, meeting and outcome logs
For expected standard, evidence should show participation in partnership networks and impact on provision

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders act in the best interest of pupils, including disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care, and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being.
key_questions: Is there evidence that the school pays specific attention to these pupils and ensures the consistency of the quality of their experience (from safeguarding to curriculum and teaching to behaviour to AP)?
suggested_evidence: Governors hold leaders to account approrpiately and effectively for these pupils
State-funded school inspection toolkit p75
The PLD programme includes documented training on adaptations needed for these pupils

Staff are able to identify disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those known (or previously known) to social care and those who may face other barriers and demonstrate how this knowledge informs planning and teaching.
School Inspection Operating Guide p14
sources: PLD programme documentation on adaptations for vulnerable pupils; Staff identification and planning records for disadvantaged/SEND/social care pupils; Governor accountability records
For expected standard, evidence should show systems in place to support vulnerable groups

Strong Standard.focus: To meet the strong standard, schools must show consistency, not pockets of good practice; precision and not general monitoring; sustained impact and not short-term gains; experience and opportunity, not just attainment.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders provide meaningful opportunities for staff and governors to share perspectives and insights and collaborate throughout any change process.
key_questions: How can these opportunities be demonstrably meaningful?
suggested_evidence: Collaboration is structured, systematic and continuous across the school.
Leaders can evidence how this input has informed decisions and led to sustained improvement rather than isolated adjustments
Leaders use established and repeated mechanisms for gathering staff and governor perspectives before, during and after change,

Expected standard shows that staff and governors are consulted; strong standard shows that collaboration is systematic, ongoing and demonstrably shapes decisions and implementation with sustained impact
sources: Structured collaboration logs (e.g. working group minutes, consultation records); Staff and governor voice surveys with analysis showing feedback shapes decisions; “You said, we did” logs showing systematic, ongoing use and demonstrable impact on decisions and outcomes; Post-implementation review records
For strong standard, evidence must show collaboration is systematic, continuous, and demonstrably shapes decisions and implementation with sustained impact, not just existence or isolated examples

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders use their detailed and insightful analysis of school performance to evaluate the effectiveness of their provision. They accurately identify, monitor and act on priorities for improvement, leading to consistently strong standards across all areas of the school’s work or rapid improvement in any areas where this is not the case.
key_questions: Is there the consistency of strength across the board? Or, if there are any areas where this is not the case, can the school clearly evidence rapid improvement and talk through a context. E.g a spike in suspensions linked to raising standards?
suggested_evidence: Leaders demonstrate a precise and insightful understanding of school performance through triangulated evidence.
Evaluation identifies underlying causes, informs sharply focused priorities and leads to sustained impact over time or clearly evidenced rapid improvement where standards are not yet strong.

Expected standard shows leaders know strengths and weaknesses; strong standard shows precise analysis, clear identification of root causes and evidence of sustained or accelerated improvement over time.
sources: Triangulated evaluation reports combining pupil, staff, governor, and external partner perspectives; Annotated SIP and SEF showing how analysis has informed sharply focused priorities and led to measurable impact; External validation reports (peer review, trust review, consultant feedback); Case studies of rapid improvement with clear context and outcomes; 3 year data showing sustained improvement or rapid progress in identified areas
For strong standard, evidence must show precise analysis, clear identification of root causes, and sustained or accelerated improvement over time, not just knowledge of strengths and weaknesses

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders at all levels make a consistently positive contribution to the school’s priorities and develop staff expertise as the key driver of improvement. They allocate enough time for staff to work together to achieve this. Staff understand their role in improving and sustaining standards and make a consistently positive contribution to this.
key_questions: Can we see the impact (even at middle leadership level) of how staff drive improvement? Has the school been strategic at providing opportunities to empower middle leaders and opportunities for staff to work together to drive improvement?
suggested_evidence: Leadership is distributed effectively and consistently.
Leaders at all levels demonstrably contribute to improvement priorities, with protected time for collaboration.
Staff can clearly articulate their role in sustaining improvement, and evidence shows this contribution leads to consistent impact across teams and phases.

Expected standard shows leaders contribute to improvement; strong standard shows consistent impact from leadership at all levels, with staff able to articulate and evidence their role in sustained improvement.
sources: Distributed leadership documentation (middle leader action plans, impact reports, staff development logs); Evidence of staff-led initiatives scaled up due to success; Staff voice surveys/interviews showing understanding of their role in improvement; Protected time for collaboration records
For strong standard, evidence must show consistent impact from leadership at all levels, with staff able to articulate and evidence their role in sustained improvement, not just contribution

judgement_areas.statement: Governors/trustees use their knowledge and expertise to provide consistent support and robust challenge to leaders across all aspect of the school’s work
suggested_evidence: Governors/trustees consistently draw on their expertise to provide robust challenge and informed support across all areas of the school’s work.
Governors' oversight is precise, evidence-informed and sustained, leading to clear impact on strategic decision-making and school improvement.
Expected standard shows governors support and challenge; strong standard shows consistently robust, expert challenge and clear evidence of impact on strategic direction and outcomes.
sources: Governor visit reports, learning walk logs, triangulation records showing robust challenge and support; Governor training logs and impact evaluations; Minutes evidencing governors’ use of expertise to probe, question, and hold leaders to account; Evidence of governors’ involvement in strategic planning and review
For strong standard, evidence must show consistently robust, expert challenge and clear evidence of impact on strategic direction and outcomes, not just support and challenge

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders have achieved a culture of high expectations and professionalism, which ensures a positive experience for all pupils.
suggested_evidence: High expectations and professionalism are consistently embedded across the school.
Evidence shows that pupils’ experiences are positive and equitable, with sustained impact on behaviour, attitudes and inclusion rather than pockets of strong practice.
Expected standard shows high expectations are articulated; strong standard shows they are consistently experienced by all pupils, with sustained positive impact across the school.
sources: Surveys and qualitative data showing a positive, inclusive culture experienced by all pupils and staff; Consistent evidence of high expectations and professionalism in all aspects of school life (e.g. behaviour logs, staff and pupil voice, visitor feedback); Case studies of how the school’s ethos has led to improved outcomes for vulnerable groups
For strong standard, evidence must show high expectations and professionalism are consistently experienced by all pupils, with sustained positive impact across the school, not just articulated

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders have developed a highly effective culture of professional learning and expertise in which staff take responsibility for their own learning and are keen to continually improve their expertise. Leaders ensure that there is a professional learning and expertise curriculum that is informed by the best available evidence, including research, and is precisely matched to priorities for whole-school improvement, subjects/teams/phases and individual needs.
key_questions: Are staff proactive at taking part in research or actively working to develop things at the school? Is there a consistency in empowerment of leadership at all levels?
suggested_evidence: Professional learning is highly effective, evidence-informed and precisely aligned to whole-school and individual priorities.
Staff take responsibility for their own development, and evaluation shows sustained improvement in practice and outcomes rather than short-term gains.
Expected standard shows a professional learning programme is in place; strong standard shows it is precisely targeted, evidence-informed and leads to sustained improvements in practice and outcomes.
sources: Professional learning curriculum mapped to school priorities, subject/phase needs, and individual staff development plans; Staff voice evidencing a culture of continuous improvement and ownership of professional development; Records of staff participation in research, action research projects, or external networks; Impact evaluations showing how professional learning has led to improved practice and outcomes
For strong standard, evidence must show professional learning is precisely targeted, evidence-informed, and leads to sustained improvements in practice and outcomes, not just programme existence

statements: Leaders do not identify and/or prioritise the right issues or take suitable or timely action to deal with them.
Leaders do not have a track record of improvement or, since being appointed, have not taken action that they could reasonably be expected to take to secure improvements.

The school’s capacity to improve is poor, or leaders depend too much on external support to bring about and/or maintain improvement. (support from within the school’s trust is not considered external support)
Governors/trustees do not carry out their statutory core functions. Breaches of legal responsibilities are serious because of the extent of their actual or potential negative impact on pupils.
The responsible body is unclear about its statutory duties in relation to leaders’ well-being and/or does not support leaders appropriately.
Staff workload is unsustainable.
Leaders’ decisions are not taken in the best interests of pupils.
Bullying, unlawful discrimination, harassment and victimisation of staff go unnoticed or unchallenged.
notes: Do any of these statements apply? If they do not, you should be looking at the needs attention criteria.

statements: Leaders have a broad awareness of the school’s strengths and areas for development, but their actions lack precision and/or do not identify the underlying causes of any weakness and/or have unintended consequences for pupils or particular groups of pupils.
Leaders’ actions to bring about improvement are largely focused on the right areas, but do not lead to improvement quickly enough.

Governors/trustees understand their broad roles and responsibilities but do not typically support and/or challenge leaders well. Their approaches to assuring themselves of the impact of leaders’ work do not give them a precise picture of it.
Leaders are conscious of the implications any changes to practice may have on staff’s workload, but they do not do enough to manage that workload or to avoid unnecessary burdens.

Leaders have a broad programme of professional learning and expertise, but it is overly generic or insufficiently targeted to build expertise (in terms of knowledge, skill and judgement). This limits its impact on the quality of the provision pupils receive. It provides few opportunities for purposeful collaboration or for staff to receive effective feedback on their practice.
Leaders have engaged with parents and the local community but this has been limited and/or not targeted appropriately.
notes: These are examples; they do not form statements like the expected standard does. There is no suggested evidence here, as what is listed provides examples of why the expected standard might not be met. Instead, use the expected standard to determine whether the school meets the required criteria. If a school does not meet one or more expected standard statements, it is considered to need attention (NA) in that area, unless an Urgent Improvement statement fully applies, in which case the school is graded as Urgent Improvement (UI). Needs Attention is not a grade criterion, but a guide to help identify areas where practice falls short of the expected standard and to support targeted improvement.

statements: Exceptionally high standards of leadership and governance have been sustained. Governors, other leaders at all levels, and all staff make a strong, positive contribution to the school’s strategic priorities. This leads to continued improvement and/or sustained high standards in all areas of the school's work.

Leaders’ actions have a transformational impact on the outcomes and experiences of disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children's social care, and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being. These pupils achieve and thrive.
There are no significant areas for improvement that leaders have not already prioritised
notes: Are leaders able to clearly articulate what is actually transformational or exceptional about what the school does? Can we see this reflected in first-hand evidence, including outcomes? is there clear evidence of how the 'transformational' part applies to vulnerable groups?

Expected Standard.focus: Expected standard focuses on systems being in place, statutory compliance, and regular review

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders have an accurate and informed understanding of the quality of the curriculum and teaching across the school. They draw on this when deciding how to deploy staff and allocate resources, and to identify when timely action is needed to bring about improvement
key_questions:
Do leaders have an accurate understanding of the quality of curriculum and teaching across the school?
What evidence do they draw on (what is their benchmark and how have they QA'd this) to form this view?
How well is this information triangulated and quality assured?
suggested_evidence: Leaders review the quality of curriculum and teaching using a range of information, including pupil outcomes, teaching evidence and staff feedback, to maintain an accurate understanding of provision
This understanding informs decisions about staff deployment and resource allocation,
Leaders take action when provision falls short.

Evidence shows leaders’ knowledge is accurate and used to inform improvement. Leaders understand how the quality of curriculum and teaching contributes to pupils’ learning over time and use this insight to evaluate impact, rather than relying on outcomes alone
sources: Lesson visit records; work scrutiny; pupil voice; assessment data; staff feedback; curriculum reviews; line management records; SEF; School Improvement Plan (SIP); monitoring schedules
For expected standard, evidence should demonstrate that leaders use a range of information to maintain an accurate understanding of curriculum and teaching and take appropriate action where needed

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders ensure that the curriculum is suitable and well planned for each subject and year group. It identifies clear end points and is appropriately sequenced to build on what has already been taught and learned.
key_questions:
Are curriculum plans clear, coherent and in place across all subjects and year groups?
Do these plans identify clear end points and build on prior learning? How has the school developed this and ensured that curriculum quality is consistent across subjects (links to development of middle leaders)?
suggested_evidence: Curriculum plans are in place for all subjects and year groups
The curriculum plans have clear end points and sequencing that builds on prior learning
Leaders review curriculum plans to ensure suitability and coherence

Leaders ensure staff understand curriculum intent. Leaders understand how curriculum sequencing enables pupils to build knowledge over time and review its impact to ensure it supports secure learning.
sources: Curriculum plans; subject overviews; medium-term plans; progression maps; schemes of work; subject leader reviews; curriculum documentation; lesson visit records
For expected standard, evidence should show that curriculum plans are in place, clearly sequenced and consistently understood by staff across subjects and year groups

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders ensure that the curriculum is generally taught well. Teachers draw on their knowledge of pupils’ needs and starting points and an evidence-informed understanding of effective teaching and how pupils learn.
key_questions:
Is teaching generally effective across the school?
What evidence shows this (for example, pupils’ work, lesson visits and pupil voice)?
Can pupils talk confidently about what they have learned, where appropriate?
suggested_evidence: Leaders monitor teaching quality
Leaders ensure teachers use knowledge of pupils’ starting points and needs to inform planning and delivery
Teaching is generally effective and responsive

Leaders provide support where improvement is needed. Leaders recognise that improvements in teaching may take time to be reflected in pupils’ outcomes and monitor impact over time using a range of evidence.
sources: Lesson visit records; work scrutiny; pupil voice; assessment information; teaching observations; feedback records; coaching records; staff feedback
For expected standard, evidence should show that teaching is generally effective, informed by pupils’ starting points, and supported where improvement is needed.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders make sure that teachers have, or gain, the expertise they need for the subjects and phases they teach.
key_questions:
Do teachers have the subject and phase expertise they need? How do leaders identify where expertise needs to be developed? What does the training and CPD programme at the school look like?
What evidence shows that professional learning is improving practice and that it is therefore effective?
suggested_evidence: Leaders identify teachers’ subject and phase expertise and provide access to appropriate professional learning where gaps are identified
Evidence shows expertise is reviewed and developed over time.
sources: CPD programme documentation; training logs; staff development plans; subject leader records; mentoring records; ECT documentation; appraisal records
For expected standard, evidence should show that leaders identify and address gaps in staff expertise through appropriate professional development.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders ensure that all pupils who are at the early stages of learning to read are taught to do so through systematic synthetic phonics
key_questions:
Are pupils at the early stages of reading taught through a systematic synthetic phonics programme?
How consistently is this delivered in practice?
How do leaders ensure that pupils who fall behind (or start behind) catch up quickly?
suggested_evidence: Leaders ensure pupils at the early stages of learning to read are taught using a systematic synthetic phonics programme, delivered with fidelity to the chosen approach
Monitoring shows phonics teaching is in place and reviewed.
sources: Phonics programme documentation; lesson visit records; phonics assessments; pupil reading records; intervention logs; training records; fidelity monitoring records
For expected standard, evidence should show that a systematic synthetic phonics programme is in place and implemented consistently, with support for pupils who fall behind.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders and staff are particularly aware of pupils who have not yet secured the necessary foundations in communication and language, reading, spelling, handwriting and mathematics. They take appropriate action to secure this foundational knowledge
key_questions:
Do leaders and staff identify pupils who have not secured key foundational knowledge?
How well do they understand where gaps exist (foresic approach to the foundations)?
What action is taken to address these, and how do leaders know it is working?
suggested_evidence: Leaders and staff identify pupils who have not secured foundational knowledge
Leaders take appropriate action to address gaps, informed by assessment information
Support is reviewed to ensure it meets pupils’ needs. Leaders understand how securing this foundational knowledge underpins pupils’ ability to access the wider curriculum and make progress over time.
sources: Assessment data; intervention records; provision maps; work scrutiny; pupil voice; support plans; progress review meetings; curriculum plans
For expected standard, evidence should show that gaps in foundational knowledge are identified and addressed through appropriate and reviewed support.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders and staff generally use assessment well to check understanding and make changes to teaching and/or the curriculum, as necessary.
key_questions:
Is assessment used effectively to check pupils’ understanding?
How well do teachers identify and address misconceptions? 
What training (CPD) supports this, and how do leaders know it is effective?
suggested_evidence: Assessment is used to check pupils’ understanding and inform adjustments to teaching and curriculum

Leaders review assessment information to support improvement. Leaders use assessment to understand how well pupils are learning the intended curriculum over time, rather than relying solely on outcome measures
sources: Assessment records; work scrutiny; lesson visit records; pupil voice; CPD logs; training materials; moderation records; feedback policies; assessment reviews
For expected standard, evidence should show that assessment is used to check understanding and inform teaching, with staff supported through training to identify and address misconceptions.

Strong Standard.focus: To meet the strong standard, schools must show consistency, not pockets of good practice; precision and not general monitoring; sustained impact and not short-term gains; experience and opportunity, not just attainment.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders make astute decisions about how the curriculum and teaching should adapt and evolve, based on their evidence and insight about how well pupils have learned what was intended.
key_questions: Can the school show consistency, not pockets of good practice; precision and not general monitoring; sustained impact and not short-term gains?
suggested_evidence: Leaders draw on a detailed and precise understanding of how well pupils have learned the intended curriculum, informed by assessment information, work scrutiny, lesson visits, and pupil voice
Evidence shows that leaders use this insight to adapt and refine curriculum content, sequencing and teaching approaches, rather than simply reviewing provision.

Decisions to adapt teaching or curriculum are clearly linked to identified learning strengths and gaps and lead to demonstrable improvements in pupils’ learning over time. Leaders recognise the relationship between curriculum, teaching and outcomes, ensuring that improvements are sustained and reflected in pupils’ learning rather than short-term performance gains.
For the strong standard evidence shows astute, insight‑driven adaptation that is sustained and embedded
sources: Triangulated evaluation reports; systematic work scrutiny; repeated lesson visit records; pupil voice across phases; curriculum evaluation documentation; annotated SEF; SIP reviews showing impact; longitudinal assessment analysis

For strong standard, evidence must show precise, triangulated evaluation that is used to adapt curriculum and teaching, resulting in sustained improvements in pupils’ learning over time, rather than isolated or short-term gains

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders ensure that the curriculum is of a consistently high quality across subjects and year groups. Leaders have a sophisticated understanding of the differences between subjects, so that pupils’ learning at each stage can be secured quickly and shaped carefully in the anticipation of future learning.
suggested_evidence: Leaders evaluate curriculum quality across all subjects and year groups, demonstrating a secure and sophisticated understanding of subject‑specific knowledge, progression and pedagogy

Evidence shows that leaders recognise and respond to differences between subjects, ensuring that curriculum design and teaching secure learning at each stage and deliberately prepare pupils for future learning.
Curriculum quality is consistently high rather than variable, and leaders’ subject insight informs decisions about sequencing, emphasis and resourcing.
For strong standard the curriculum is showing consistency, subject sophistication and anticipation of future learning needs
sources: Whole-school curriculum documentation; subject reviews across all subjects; progression mapping across phases; repeated lesson visits; systematic work scrutiny; subject leader reports; external quality assurance records

For strong standard, evidence must show consistently high-quality curriculum design across all subjects and phases, informed by secure subject expertise and resulting in coherent, cumulative learning for pupils.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders ensure that the curriculum is consistently taught well. Highly effective teaching is embedded across subjects and year groups. Teaching ensures that pupils consistently develop their language and vocabulary, both spoken and written, and increase their reading competency, across subjects.
suggested_evidence: Monitoring evidence shows that teaching is consistently effective across subjects and year groups, with limited variability
Leaders ensure that teaching approaches systematically develop pupils’ language, vocabulary and reading competency across the curriculum, not only in English.
Evidence demonstrates that this focus is embedded in everyday classroom practice and sustained over time, resulting in improved access to learning across subjects.
Strong standard evidence shows embedded, highly effective practice with consistent impact across the school.
sources: Repeated lesson visit records; systematic work scrutiny; pupil voice across subjects; assessment analysis over time; monitoring reports showing consistency; coaching and feedback records; CPD impact evaluations

For strong standard, evidence must show highly effective teaching embedded consistently across subjects and year groups, with sustained impact on pupils’ knowledge, understanding and application over time.

judgement_areas.statement: Teachers consistently make highly effective choices about what to teach, and when and how to teach it, in the context of the subject, phase and pupils’ needs.
suggested_evidence: Teachers demonstrate consistently strong professional judgement in selecting curriculum content and teaching approaches that are well matched to subject demands, phase expectations and pupils’ needs
Evidence shows that teachers adapt teaching with precision, informed by assessment and subject knowledge, and that these decisions lead to secure learning and progression.
This is evident across subjects and phases rather than in isolated examples.
Strong standard evidence shows consistency and effectiveness in teachers’ curricular and pedagogical choices, rather than general responsiveness seen at expected standard.
sources: Lesson visit records across subjects; systematic work scrutiny; assessment records informing teaching; planning documentation; subject leader monitoring; teaching evaluations over time; coaching records demonstrating impact

For strong standard, evidence must show that teachers make consistently precise and effective decisions about what, when and how to teach, informed by secure subject knowledge and assessment, leading to secure learning across the curriculum.

judgement_areas.statement: The school’s approach to the curriculum and teaching for disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care, and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being is implemented consistently well across the school to remove barriers to achievement for these pupils.
suggested_evidence: Leaders ensure that curriculum design and teaching approaches for vulnerable pupils are consistently implemented across subjects and year groups, rather than dependent on individual teachers or teams
Evidence shows that barriers to learning are anticipated and addressed through curriculum planning and teaching, enabling these pupils to access ambitious learning and make strong progress.
Monitoring demonstrates consistency and sustained impact across the school.
Strong standard evidence shows school‑wide consistency and effectiveness in removing barriers to achievement.
sources: Provision maps; intervention records over time; SEND and inclusion plans; pupil premium strategies; work scrutiny for targeted groups; lesson visits across subjects; inclusion reviews; case studies; progress data over time

For strong standard, evidence must show that adaptations and support for vulnerable pupils are implemented consistently across the school, remove barriers to learning, and result in sustained improvement in pupils’ knowledge and participation over time.

statements: Leaders’ understanding of the quality of the curriculum and/or teaching is inaccurate.
Leaders are not doing enough to tackle weaknesses in pupils’ education.
The curriculum lacks ambition, structure or coherence. Leaders have not considered its content and sequencing sufficiently.
The range of subjects is limited and narrow. It does not prepare pupils for adulthood or provide them with the knowledge they need toachieve and thrive in later life.

The curriculum lacks ambition for disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children’s socialcare, and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being.
Pupils with SEND do not receive a high-quality education and/or reasonable adjustments are not being made.
Pupils' experiences in lessons do not help them to learn effectively what has been planned in the curriculum.
Leaders do not do enough to make sure that teachers develop sufficient expertise to teach the curriculum.
Teachers' expectations are too low, either for all pupils or for particular groups.
Adaptations or reasonable adjustments do not meet pupils' needs or they reflect low expectations for particular pupils or groups
notes: Do any of these statements apply? If they do not, you should be looking at the needs attention criteria.

statements: Leaders have only recently started to take appropriate action to identify and assess pupils’ needs or reduce barriers to pupils’ learning and/or well-being.
Weaknesses or inconsistencies in practice have a negative impact on a particular group of pupils.
Leaders’ use of alternative provision has limited positive impact on pupils’ learning and/or well-being.
notes: These are examples; they do not form statements like the expected standard does. There is no suggested evidence here, as what is listed provides examples of why the expected standard might not be met. Instead, use the expected standard to determine whether the school meets the required criteria. If a school does not meet one or more expected standard statements, it is considered to need attention (NA) in that area, unless an Urgent Improvement statement fully applies, in which case the school is graded as Urgent Improvement (UI). Needs Attention is not a grade criterion, but a guide to help identify areas where practice falls short of the expected standard and to support targeted improvement.

statements: Exceptionally high standards in the curriculum and teaching have been sustained. Across all subjects and phases, well-planned, effectiveand highly responsive teaching quickly and securely develops the knowledge and skills pupils need to be successful.

Leaders' actions have a transformational impact on the learning of disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (orpreviously known) to children's social care, and those who may face other barriers to learning and/or well-being. These pupils are nowexceptionally well prepared for current and later learning, and for future success.
There are no significant areas for improvement that leaders have not already prioritised.
notes:
Are leaders able to clearly articulate what is actually transformational or exceptional about curriculum and teaching? What is truly exceptional about how the curriculum and teaching remove barriers for disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those known (or previously known) to children’s social care, or those facing other barriers – particularly where leaders and staff have gone to exceptional lengths to secure pupils’ learning and future success?
3. Is there evidence that exceptionally high standards of curriculum and teaching have been sustained across all subjects and phases, with no significant areas for improvement that leaders have not already identified and prioritised? The school will probably already have done work to model the provision as best practice in the region, or be at the stage where they could.

Expected Standard.focus: Expected standard focuses on systems being in place, statutory compliance, and regular review

judgement_areas.statement: On the whole, pupils are ready for the next stage of education, employment or training. They generally have appropriate knowledge and skills across the curriculum, as reflected in the quality of their responses and the work they produce.
suggested_evidence: Leaders evaluate whether most pupils are ready for the next stage by reviewing the quality of pupils’ work across subjects, pupils’ responses in lessons and patterns in outcomes over time.

Leaders use attainment information, internal tracking and destination data to confirm that pupils generally have the appropriate knowledge and skills to move successfully into further education, employment or training.

Evidence is triangulated with first‑hand curriculum and teaching evaluation rather than relying solely on headline outcomes. Leaders interpret this evidence in context, including levels of disadvantage and cohort characteristics, and compare groups appropriately (for example, disadvantaged pupils with disadvantaged pupils nationally) to ensure judgements are fair and secure

Leaders recognise that improvements in curriculum and teaching may not yet be fully reflected in outcomes and use a range of evidence to maintain an accurate understanding of pupils’ readiness for the next stage

judgement_areas.statement: Pupils develop the foundational knowledge and skills they need, including language and communication skills. Pupils who are at the start of their education (and older pupils, where necessary) largely secure the necessary accuracy and fluency in word reading, spelling, handwriting and number facts. Any gaps in pupils’ foundational knowledge or skills are closing quickly.
suggested_evidence: Leaders use assessment information and classroom evidence to check that pupils develop secure foundations in language, communication, reading, spelling, handwriting and number facts.
Leaders monitor pupils at the earliest stages of learning and older pupils where needed, ensuring that gaps in foundational knowledge are identified quickly and addressed through timely support.

Evidence shows leaders routinely review whether gaps are closing over time, rather than only identifying them, and understand how improvements in these foundations contribute to pupils’ later achievement.

judgement_areas.statement: On the whole, pupils achieve well. Typically, this will be reflected in their attainment and progress in national tests and examinations, which are broadly in line with national averages, including for disadvantaged pupils.
suggested_evidence: Leaders analyse attainment and progress in national tests and examinations alongside internal performance information to confirm that, on the whole, pupils achieve well and outcomes are broadly in line with national averages.
The school is broadly in line with national average, as indicated by the IDSR, for the last year
The IDSR highlights that the school has a positive three year trend, as highlighted on the IDSR (static or improving)
Leaders review trends over time rather than isolated results, including cohort and subject-level patterns, using this analysis to understand strengths and areas for development across the curriculum.

Leaders interpret outcomes in the context of the school, including pupil characteristics such as disadvantage, to ensure that conclusions about achievement are accurate and appropriately contextualised.

judgement_areas.statement: Disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care, and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being generally make suitable progress from their starting points. They develop appropriate knowledge and skills to enable them to progress to the next stage. Any gaps in their knowledge or skills are closing quickly
suggested_evidence: Leaders track the progress of disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND and other vulnerable groups from their starting points using assessment information, progress reviews and subject‑level analysis.
Evidence shows that these pupils generally make suitable progress and develop the knowledge and skills needed for the next stage.
Leaders monitor whether gaps are closing over time and adjust support where progress is slower, rather than relying on assumptions of improvement
Leaders use assessment and progress information alongside first-hand evidence to confirm that these pupils develop appropriate knowledge and skills to move on to the next stage.

Leaders consider outcomes for these groups in context and compare them appropriately (for example, disadvantaged pupils with disadvantaged pupils nationally), ensuring that evaluation is based on secure and fair evidence

Strong Standard.focus: To meet the strong standard, schools must show consistency, not pockets of good practice; precision and not general monitoring; sustained impact and not short-term gains; experience and opportunity, not just attainment.

judgement_areas.statement: Pupils, including disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care, and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being, consistently achieve well, develop detailed knowledge and skills, and produce high-quality work across the curriculum.
key_questions: Can the school show consistency, not pockets of good practice; precision and not general monitoring; sustained impact and not short-term gains?
suggested_evidence: Leaders monitor progress and attainment across all subjects and phases, confirming that all pupil groups—including disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, and other vulnerable groups—consistently achieve well and develop detailed knowledge and skills.
Work scrutiny, lesson visits, and assessment data show that pupils produce high-quality work and demonstrate secure understanding across the curriculum.
Leaders triangulate evidence from national assessments, internal tracking, and subject reviews to ensure consistency and depth of achievement
Leaders’ analysis takes full account of the school’s context, demonstrating that strong achievement is sustained across groups, including where there are higher levels of disadvantage.

For strong standard, evidence must show that this is consistent across all groups and subjects, with high-quality work and secure knowledge demonstrated in multiple forms of evidence, not just headline data

judgement_areas.statement: Typically, this achievement will be reflected in above-average outcomes in national tests and examinations over time, including for disadvantaged pupils. Any gaps are quickly narrowing.
suggested_evidence: Attainment and progress data from national tests and examinations show that pupils, including disadvantaged pupils, achieve above national averages over time.
Leaders analyse trends and cohort-level data to confirm sustained high achievement and rapid narrowing of gaps between groups.
Published performance tables, internal tracking, and progress meetings are used to evidence improvement and consistency.

Leaders demonstrate that outcomes for key groups compare favourably with appropriate national benchmarks (for example, disadvantaged pupils compared with disadvantaged pupils nationally), and that gaps are closing rapidly and consistently over time.
For strong standard, evidence must show sustained above-average outcomes and that gaps are closing rapidly and consistently, not just improving in isolated years or subjects.

judgement_areas.statement: All pupils, including the groups above, are consistently well prepared for the next stage in their education, employment or training.
suggested_evidence: Leaders track all pupils’ readiness for the next stage through transition records, destination data, and feedback from further education providers and employers (ie. Range of sources).
Leaders use work scrutiny, lesson visits, and pupil voice to confirm that pupils consistently have the knowledge, skills, and confidence needed for future learning or employment
Evidence shows that pupils across all groups are consistently well prepared for the next stage, with readiness reflected in both outcomes and the quality of pupils’ work and understanding.
For strong standard, evidence must show that all groups are consistently well prepared, with transition and destination data confirming readiness across the school, not just for the majority

statements: Pupils lack the foundations of communication, reading, writing or mathematical knowledge (taking into account that some pupils with SENDmay not establish these foundations). Gaps in foundational knowledge are not tackled quickly or effectively.
Pupils have not gained the knowledge and skills and/or any qualifications they need to progress to the next stage of education, training oremployment.
The progress that disadvantaged pupils make is consistently well below that of other pupils nationally and shows little or no improvement.
notes: Do any of these statements apply? If they do not, you should be looking at the needs attention criteria.

statements: Although many pupils achieve well, a significant minority have gaps in their learning, which hinders their achievement.

Leaders have only recently started to take action to improve achievement. While their actions are appropriate, they are at an early stage. This means it is too soon to determine the impact of this work.
Pupils' attainment and progress are inconsistent or not as good as they need to be over time.

Leaders are taking appropriate action to improve the achievement of disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care, and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being. However, currently, a significant minority of these pupils do not achieve well from their starting points.
notes: These are examples; they do not form statements like the expected standard does. There is no suggested evidence here, as what is listed provides examples of why the expected standard might not be met. Instead, use the expected standard to determine whether the school meets the required criteria. If a school does not meet one or more expected standard statements, it is considered to need attention (NA) in that area, unless an Urgent Improvement statement fully applies, in which case the school is graded as Urgent Improvement (UI). Needs Attention is not a grade criterion, but a guide to help identify areas where practice falls short of the expected standard and to support targeted improvement.

statements: Exceptionally high standards of achievement have been sustained. Across all subjects and phases, pupils achieve consistently well,developing detailed knowledge and skills. This will be reflected in consistently high outcomes in national tests and examinations (whereavailable).

Leaders' actions have a transformational impact on the achievement of disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (orpreviously known) to children’s social care, and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being. These pupils nowachieve consistently high outcomes and are exceptionally well prepared for their next steps. The difference between the attainment rate ofthe school’s disadvantaged pupils and that of all non-disadvantaged pupils nationally is far narrower over time than the difference betweenthe attainment rate of all disadvantaged pupils nationally and that of non-disadvantaged pupils nationally.
There are no significant areas for improvement that leaders have not already prioritised.
notes: Are leaders able to clearly articulate what is actually transformational or exceptional about what the school does? What is truly special about what the school does in terms of how they might have gone to exceptional lengths to overcome barriers or provide a specialist provision that is truly transformational in terms of those pupils life chances? The school will probably already have done work to model the provision as best practice in the region, or be at the stage where they could.

Expected Standard.focus: Expected standard focuses on systems being in place, statutory compliance, and regular review

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders and staff have an informed and accurate understanding of matters related to attendance, behaviour and attitudes. They establish effective strategies to tackle any issues.
key_questions:
Do leaders know whether attendance is in line with national averages, including for key groups?
What do patterns in attendance data show, including different attendance bands?
How well do leaders understand these patterns and use them to tailor bespoke support for pupils and families?
suggested_evidence: Leaders and staff use attendance and behaviour data, pupil voice, and staff feedback to maintain an informed and accurate understanding of patterns and issues.
They review trends over time, identify groups or individuals requiring support, and establish effective strategies that are regularly reviewed and adapted.

Evidence includes triangulation of registers, incident logs, and surveys, with strategies aligned to DfE guidance on behaviour and attendance. Leaders understand how attendance and behaviour patterns affect pupils’ access to learning and use this knowledge to inform their strategies over time.
sources: Attendance registers; attendance dashboards; behaviour logs; incident records; exclusion records; pupil voice surveys; staff surveys; attendance analysis reports; behaviour analysis reports; records of strategy reviews
For expected standard, evidence should demonstrate that leaders use a range of data and feedback to understand patterns and inform appropriate strategies.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders establish high expectations for all pupils about behaviour, built on positive relationships, and on rules and routines that staff and pupils generally understand.
key_questions:
How have high expectations been established? Are expectations for behaviour clear and well established? Are these implemented consistently in practice across the school?
How do leaders know that staff and pupils understand what is expected?
suggested_evidence: Leaders communicate high expectations for behaviour through clear policies, assemblies, and staff training, ensuring alignment with DfE statutory guidance on behaviour and Prevent duty.
Positive relationships are fostered, and rules and routines are consistently reinforced.
Evidence includes behaviour policies, staff and pupil surveys, and records of assemblies or briefings, with staff and pupils demonstrating understanding of expectations
sources: Behaviour policy; staff training records; assembly materials; behaviour expectations documentation; pupil and staff surveys; behaviour monitoring records
For expected standard, evidence should show that expectations are clearly communicated and generally understood by staff and pupils.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders ensure that staff maintain and reinforce the same high expectations, acting as role models and teaching positive behaviour.
key_questions:
Do staff actively promote and teach positive behaviour and relationships?
Are expectations applied consistently by staff across the school?
How do leaders know that this is the case?
suggested_evidence: Staff consistently model positive behaviour and reinforce expectations in lessons and around the school, in line with DfE behaviour guidance.
Leaders monitor staff practice through lesson visits, behaviour walks, and feedback from pupils.
Evidence includes staff training records, lesson observation notes, and behaviour logs showing consistent reinforcement, with staff acting as role models and pupils responding positively
sources: Lesson visit records; behaviour walk records; staff training logs; behaviour logs; pupil voice; staff feedback; monitoring records
For expected standard, evidence should show that staff generally apply expectations consistently and model positive behaviour.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders and staff generally apply agreed rules and sanctions effectively. Suspension and permanent exclusion are used appropriately.
key_questions: What do trends in suspensions and exclusions show over time? How well do staff understand and apply the behaviour policy? What is pupils' view? What is done to support pupils to improve behaviour and make positive choices? Are alternative provision and adapted timetables used appropriately?
suggested_evidence: Leaders and staff apply agreed rules and sanctions consistently, with evidence from behaviour logs, exclusion records, and incident reports.
Suspensions and permanent exclusions are used in line with DfE statutory guidance, with records showing compliance and appropriate use.

Leaders monitor and review all forms of removal from lessons, including internal exclusion, reflection rooms, or other alternatives to suspension, tracking frequency, duration, and pupil groups affected. Evidence shows that these alternatives are not used disproportionately or as a substitute for formal exclusion, and that interventions are in place to reduce repeat incidents.
Leaders review exclusion data, ensure reintegration meetings are held, and monitor the impact of interventions to reduce repeat incidents
sources: Behaviour logs; exclusion records; suspension data; incident reports; internal exclusion records; reintegration meeting records; intervention logs; alternative provision records
For expected standard, evidence should show that behaviour systems and sanctions are applied appropriately and in line with statutory guidance.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders and staff usually ensure that incidents of bullying, unlawful discrimination, harassment, victimisation, physical and/or sexual violence and derogatory language are dealt with quickly and effectively.
key_questions:
Are incidents of bullying, discrimination and harassment recorded and addressed effectively and clearly recorded? Is this explicitly taught eg. PSHE? How quickly and effectively are issues dealt with?
What does pupil voice indicate about how safe pupils feel?
suggested_evidence: Leaders and staff respond promptly to incidents of bullying, discrimination, harassment, violence, and derogatory language, in line with DfE statutory guidance and Prevent duty. Evidence includes incident logs, investigation records, and follow-up actions.
Leaders monitor patterns, review intervention effectiveness, and ensure staff receive regular safeguarding and behaviour management training
sources: Safeguarding logs; incident records; behaviour logs; anti-bullying records; investigation records; staff training logs; pupil voice surveys
For expected standard, evidence should show that incidents are recorded and typically addressed promptly and appropriately

judgement_areas.statement: Pupils generally behave well, follow the agreed school routines and show positive attitudes to their learning. This contributes to a safe and calm environment.
key_questions:
Do pupils follow routines and demonstrate positive attitudes to learning? Can this be seen consistently across the school?
How do leaders know this is typical rather than isolated?
suggested_evidence: Behaviour logs, lesson observations, and pupil voice confirm that pupils generally behave well, follow routines, and show positive attitudes to learning.
Leaders monitor behaviour trends, intervene where necessary, and ensure the environment is safe and calm, in line with DfE behaviour guidance.
Leaders understand that consistent behaviour supports sustained engagement in learning and enables pupils to focus on building knowledge and skills over time
sources: Behaviour logs; behaviour policy, pupil voice, lesson visit records; behaviour monitoring records; pupil voice; staff feedback; assembly records; routines documentation
For expected standard, evidence should show that pupils generally behave well and that a safe and calm environment is maintained.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders analyse attendance information closely, at whole-school level and for different groups to identify patterns and trends. They use this analysis well to identify the causes of poor attendance, intervene early and remove barriers.
key_questions:
Do leaders analyse attendance at whole-school and group level?
How well do they understand the causes of absence?
What action is taken to address these issues?
Can leaders demonstrate the impact of support for individual pupils over time?
suggested_evidence: Leaders analyse attendance data at whole-school and group level, identifying patterns and trends in line with DfE attendance guidance.
Early intervention strategies are implemented to address causes of poor attendance, with evidence from tracking, intervention logs, and records of meetings with pupils and families.

Leaders review the impact of interventions and adjust strategies as needed, triangulating analysis with pupil voice and staff feedback. Leaders recognise that improved attendance enables pupils to access the curriculum more consistently and use this understanding to evaluate the impact of their strategies over time.
sources: Attendance data reports; attendance tracking systems; intervention logs; meeting records with families; attendance action plans; pupil attendance case studies
For expected standard, evidence should show that leaders analyse attendance patterns and take appropriate action to address emerging issues.

judgement_areas.statement: Overall attendance is broadly in line with national averages or shows an improving trend over time.
key_questions:
Is overall attendance in line with national averages or improving over time?
What do trends over time show?
How do leaders use this information to sustain or improve attendance?
suggested_evidence: Attendance data shows that overall attendance is broadly in line with national averages or improving over time.
Leaders review published attendance figures, internal tracking, and cohort-level trends, ensuring compliance with DfE attendance guidance.
Evidence includes attendance dashboards, published performance tables, and records of attendance reviews, with strategies adjusted to maintain or improve rates
sources: Published attendance data; IDSR; attendance dashboards; internal tracking reports; cohort-level analysis; attendance review records
For expected standard, evidence should show that overall attendance is broadly in line with national averages or improving over time.

judgement_areas.statement: Attendance is improving, including the attendance of pupils who are persistently or severely absent and individuals or groups that leaders have focused on.
key_questions:
Are leaders improving attendance for persistently or severely absent pupils? Do they know the context for these individuals/groups? 
How effective are strategies for targeted groups?
What evidence shows that attendance is improving over time?
suggested_evidence: Leaders track attendance for persistently or severely absent pupils and targeted groups, using intervention logs and attendance data to confirm improvement.
Attendance trends for targeted groups are reviewed regularly, in line with DfE attendance guidance.

Leaders prioritise disadvantaged pupils and those facing barriers to attendance, recognising that absence disproportionately affects these groups and taking targeted action to remove barriers to learning over time
sources: Persistent absence data; severe absence data; intervention plans; attendance case studies; meeting records; family engagement logs; attendance reviews
For expected standard, evidence should show that leaders track and take action to improve attendance for targeted pupils and groups.

judgement_areas.statement: Any reasonable adjustments or adaptations to attendance and/or behaviour strategies are timely and appropriate, including for disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care, and those who face barriers to their learning and/or well-being, such as young carers. Any interventions are timely, well chosen and targeted.
key_questions:
Are attendance and behaviour strategies adapted for vulnerable pupils?
How well are these aligned with wider inclusion and safeguarding work to support the holistic needs of children and families?
How do leaders know these adaptations are effective?
suggested_evidence: Leaders and staff make timely and appropriate reasonable adjustments to attendance and behaviour strategies for disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, and other vulnerable groups, in line with DfE statutory guidance on behaviour, attendance, and restrictive interventions.
Evidence includes records of adaptations, intervention logs, and reviews of support plans.
