Leaders monitor the impact of interventions and adjust strategies to meet changing needs, ensuring staff are trained in statutory requirements and best practice. Leaders ensure that these adaptations enable pupils to engage with learning and access the curriculum alongside their peers over time
sources: Provision maps; attendance intervention plans; behaviour support plans; SEND records; safeguarding records; inclusion reviews; staff training records
For expected standard, evidence should show that adaptations are planned, implemented and reviewed to support vulnerable pupils appropriately.

Strong Standard.focus: To meet the strong standard, schools must show consistency, not pockets of good practice; precision and not general monitoring; sustained impact and not short-term gains; experience and opportunity, not just attainment.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders and staff have established a culture that is highly conducive to learning, in which pupils of all ages flourish. Leaders respond to the school’s and pupils’ changing circumstances and needs swiftly and skilfully.
key_questions: Can the school show consistency, not pockets of good practice; precision and not general monitoring; sustained impact and not short-term gains?
suggested_evidence: Leaders and staff have embedded a culture of high expectations and positive relationships, evidenced by consistently calm, orderly environments and strong pupil engagement across all ages.
Behaviour and attendance data, lesson observations, pupil voice, and staff feedback confirm that pupils flourish and barriers to learning are swiftly addressed.
Leaders demonstrate agility in responding to changing circumstances, adapting strategies and support promptly, with records showing sustained impact and improvement.
Evidence includes behaviour logs, attendance reviews, intervention records, and documentation of rapid response to emerging needs, all aligned with DfE statutory guidance (
For strong standard, evidence must show sustained, school-wide impact and rapid, skilful adaptation to changing needs, not just compliance or isolated pockets of good practice
sources: Behaviour and attendance analysis over time; repeated lesson visit records; systematic behaviour monitoring; pupil voice across groups; staff feedback over time; intervention records showing impact

For strong standard, evidence must show consistent, school-wide practice, informed by triangulated evidence and resulting in sustained improvements in pupils’ behaviour, attendance and engagement over time.

judgement_areas.statement: Pupils show self-discipline and dedication to their learning. They learn how to manage their own emotions and resolve conflict with others. They consistently show high levels of respect for others and rarely need to be reminded about positive behaviour. Older pupils’ behaviour, including consideration for others, sets an excellent example.
suggested_evidence: Behaviour logs, lesson observations, and pupil voice demonstrate that pupils consistently show self-discipline, dedication to learning, and high levels of respect for others.
Evidence shows pupils manage their emotions and resolve conflict independently, with minimal reminders needed.
Older pupils act as role models, setting excellent examples for younger pupils.
Leaders monitor behaviour trends and provide targeted support, including restorative approaches and peer mentoring, with records showing sustained improvement and positive culture.
Staff training and pupil programmes are aligned with DfE behaviour guidance and Prevent duty
For strong standard, evidence must show consistent self-discipline, independence, and exemplary behaviour across all ages, not just compliance or staff-led reminders
sources: Behaviour logs over time; repeated lesson visits; pupil voice across phases; incident analysis; mentoring and intervention records; behaviour trend data
For strong standard, evidence must show that high standards of behaviour are consistently demonstrated by pupils across the school, with sustained impact rather than reliance on staff intervention.

judgement_areas.statement: Tailored and responsive reasonable adjustments and adaptations, interventions and/or support for pupils who need help to improve their behaviour have a demonstrable impact.
suggested_evidence: Leaders and staff provide tailored, responsive adjustments and interventions for pupils needing support with behaviour, including disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, and other vulnerable groups.

Evidence includes records of individual support plans, intervention logs, reviews of impact, and documentation of reasonable adjustments in line with DfE behaviour, attendance, and restrictive intervention guidance.
Leaders monitor the effectiveness of interventions, ensuring sustained improvement and positive outcomes for targeted pupils.
Staff training records show awareness of statutory requirements and best practice.

For strong standard, evidence must show interventions are precisely tailored, responsive, and demonstrably impactful, with sustained improvement for all targeted pupils, not just initial or isolated gains
sources: Individual behaviour plans; intervention records over time; provision maps; monitoring records; case studies; review meeting records; staff training logs
For strong standard, evidence must show that support is precisely tailored, consistently implemented and leads to sustained improvement in behaviour over time.

judgement_areas.statement: The importance of high attendance is consistently promoted well by leaders and staff. High-quality training and support enable them to have supportive but challenging conversations with pupils and families when attendance needs to improve.
suggested_evidence: Leaders and staff consistently promote the importance of high attendance through assemblies, communications, and daily practice.
Evidence includes records of attendance campaigns, parent communications, and pupil assemblies.
Staff receive high-quality training and support, enabling them to conduct supportive but challenging conversations with pupils and families, in line with DfE attendance guidance.
Leaders monitor the impact of these conversations, with records showing improved attendance and engagement.
Attendance strategies are reviewed and adapted to ensure sustained improvement and positive culture
For strong standard, evidence must show consistent, high-quality promotion and sustained impact, with staff empowered to challenge and support effectively, not just compliance or periodic campaigns
sources: Attendance strategy documentation; staff training records; communication logs with families; attendance campaign records; monitoring records of conversations; attendance trend data

For strong standard, evidence must show that attendance is consistently promoted through well-embedded practice, with staff effectively supporting and challenging pupils and families to secure sustained improvement.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders' actions to identify and tackle barriers to attendance result in high attendance or rapid and/or notable improvement, both overall and for individuals and groups
suggested_evidence: Leaders use detailed attendance analysis to identify and tackle barriers for all pupils, including persistently or severely absent groups. Evidence includes attendance tracking, intervention logs, records of meetings with families, and reviews of impact.
Leaders’ actions result in high attendance or rapid, notable improvement, confirmed by sustained trends in attendance data and positive feedback from pupils and families.
Strategies are aligned with DfE attendance guidance and are reviewed for effectiveness, ensuring improvement is consistent across all groups and not limited to isolated cases

For strong standard, evidence must show high attendance or rapid, sustained improvement for all groups, with barriers addressed proactively and impact demonstrated over time, not just short-term gains or improvement for the majority.
sources: Attendance data over time; persistent absence tracking; intervention records; case studies; cohort and group analysis; review cycle documentation; family engagement records

For strong standard, evidence must show that leaders’ actions result in high attendance or rapid and sustained improvement across all groups, with barriers addressed systematically and impact demonstrated over time.

statements: Leaders’ expectations of pupils’ attendance, behaviour and attitudes are not high enough. This negatively affects pupils’ achievement, theirenjoyment and their sense of belonging at school.

Leaders do not take effective action to secure pupils’ positive attendance and/or behaviour. They do not have a consistent, whole-schoolapproach to maintaining discipline. Support for staff to manage behaviour is weak.

Leaders do not have a strategic approach to tackling poor attendance. They do not monitor or evaluate pupils’ attendance properly,including at alternative provision, to identify accurately the barriers to improving attendance and to establish and implement a clear actionplan.
Attendance is consistently low. It shows little or no sign of sustained improvement over time, overall or for significant groups.
A significant minority of pupils show a lack of respect for each other and/or staff and a lack of self-discipline. Pupils frequently ignore orrefuse staff’s requests to moderate their conduct.
Incidents of bullying and/or prejudiced and discriminatory behaviour, both direct and indirect, are frequent and/or tolerated.
Pupils’ lack of engagement and persistent, low-level and/or high-level disruption contribute to reduced learning and/or disorderly conduct,within classrooms and/or around the school.

Leaders do not take pupils’ concerns seriously. Problems escalate because leaders act too slowly. Pupils have little confidence in the school’sability to tackle bullying, unlawful discrimination, harassment, victimisation, violence and/or discriminatory behaviour.
Use of force or restrictive intervention is excessive and/or unsafe.
Sanctions, suspensions and/or permanent exclusions are used inappropriately
notes: Do any of these statements apply? If they do not, you should be looking at the needs attention criteria.

statements: Leaders have appropriate ambitions and/or expectations for pupils' attendance, behaviour and attitudes, but weaknesses or inconsistencies in practice mean that these have a limited impact on pupils or a particular group of pupils.

Leaders have only recently started to take action to improv pupils' attendance, behaviour and/or attitudes. While their actions are appropriate, they are at an early stage. This means it is too soon to determine the impact of this work.

Support for disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to hildren's social care, and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being is not improving their attendnace, behaviour, and/or attitudes to learning. Support is not adapted to keep up with pupils' changing circumstances.
notes: These are examples; they do not form statements like the expected standard does. There is no suggested evidence here, as what is listed provides examples of why the expected standard might not be met. Instead, use the expected standard to determine whether the school meets the required criteria. If a school does not meet one or more expected standard statements, it is considered to need attention (NA) in that area, unless an Urgent Improvement statement fully applies, in which case the school is graded as Urgent Improvement (UI). Needs Attention is not a grade criterion, but a guide to help identify areas where practice falls short of the expected standard and to support targeted improvement.

statements: Exceptional standards of attendance and behaviour have been sustained over time so that there is a highly inclusive culture in which allpupils feel that they belong. Pupils who need it receive exceptional and tailored support from highly skilled staff to improve their attendanceand behaviour, with perceptible results.

Leaders’ actions have a transformational impact on how well disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previouslyknown) to children’s social care, and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being achieve and flourish across allareas of school life.
There are no significant areas for improvement that leaders have not already prioritised
notes: Are leaders able to clearly articulate what is actually transformational or exceptional about what the school does? What is truly special about what the school does in terms of how they might have gone to exceptional lengths to overcome barriers or provide a specialist provision that is truly transformational in terms of those pupils life chances? The school will probably already have done work to model the provision as best practice in the region, or be at the stage where they could.

judgement_areas.statement: A coherent and appropriate programme of personal development extends across the taught curriculum and wider opportunities and experiences. It makes a positive difference to pupils and enables them to develop spiritually, morally, socially and culturally.
suggested_evidence: Leaders ensure a coherent and appropriate programme of personal development is embedded across the taught curriculum and wider opportunities, including enrichment activities, assemblies, and community engagement.
Evidence includes curriculum maps, records of enrichment participation, pupil voice, and impact reviews showing positive difference in pupils’ spiritual, moral, social and cultural development.
Leaders triangulate feedback from pupils, staff, and parents to confirm the programme’s effectiveness and regularly review provision to ensure statutory compliance and responsiveness to pupil needs

judgement_areas.statement: The personal development programme includes a suitable and well taught RHE/RSHE programme, which develops pupils’ knowledge.
suggested_evidence: Leaders ensure the personal development programme includes a suitable and well-taught RHE/RSHE curriculum, fully compliant with the 2026 DfE statutory guidance.
Evidence includes curriculum plans, lesson observations, staff training records, and pupil feedback confirming that pupils’ knowledge is developed appropriately.

Leaders ensure that the personal development programme, including RSHE/PSHE, is delivered by staff with appropriate subject knowledge and confidence. Where non-specialists teach these subjects, leaders provide targeted professional development and monitor the quality of teaching and its impact on pupils’ knowledge and understanding.
Leaders review the RHE/RSHE programme for statutory compliance, including coverage of all required content, age-appropriate delivery, parental engagement, and safeguarding.
Provision is responsive to emerging issues such as online safety, healthy relationships, and mental health

judgement_areas.statement: Pupils develop their understanding of, and respect for, protected characteristics, fundamental British values and cultural diversity in modern Britain.
suggested_evidence: Leaders ensure the curriculum and wider programme develop pupils’ understanding of and respect for protected characteristics, fundamental British values, and cultural diversity.
Evidence includes curriculum plans, lesson observations, records of assemblies and events, and pupil voice confirming understanding and respect.
Leaders monitor the impact through surveys and incident logs, ensuring compliance with statutory guidance and responsiveness to local context

judgement_areas.statement: Pupils have a range of suitable opportunities that broaden their experiences and enable them to develop their talents and interests in areas such as the arts, music and sport.
suggested_evidence: Leaders provide a range of suitable opportunities for pupils to broaden their experiences and develop talents and interests in the arts, music, sport, and other enrichment activities. Evidence includes records of participation, enrichment programme maps, pupil voice, and impact reviews.
Leaders track participation and review provision to ensure accessibility and responsiveness to pupil interests and needs

judgement_areas.statement: The school’s careers education, where relevant, prepares pupils for future education, employment or training. The school is making steady progress towards the Gatsby benchmarks.
suggested_evidence: Leaders ensure the school’s careers education prepares pupils for future education, employment, or training, with evidence of progress towards the Gatsby benchmarks.

The school complies with the Baker Clause, publishing a provider access policy and granting access to a range of education and training providers for pupils in Years 8 to 13. Evidence includes curriculum plans, records of careers events, pupil feedback, engagement with external providers, and destination data.
Leaders monitor the impact of careers education through reviews of provision and compliance with statutory requirements

judgement_areas.statement: Effective pastoral support meets pupils’ needs. They are confident in accessing it when they need it.
suggested_evidence: Leaders ensure effective pastoral support is available and meets pupils’ needs, with evidence from pupil voice, pastoral logs, and records of interventions.
Pupils are confident in accessing support, confirmed by surveys and feedback.
Leaders review pastoral provision for quality, accessibility, and responsiveness to pupil needs, ensuring compliance with statutory guidance and safeguarding requirements

judgement_areas.statement: The personal development programme is an entitlement for every pupil. Leaders track participation. They take steps to ensure that pupils, including disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care, and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being, for example young carers, can participate appropriately. Reasonable adjustments or adaptations are made for them.
suggested_evidence: Leaders ensure the personal development programme is an entitlement for every pupil, tracking participation and making reasonable adjustments for disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, vulnerable groups, and young carers.
Evidence includes participation logs, records of adaptations, intervention reviews, and pupil voice confirming accessibility.
Leaders monitor impact and review provision to ensure all pupils can participate fully, in line with statutory guidance and best practice

judgement_areas.statement: Pupils develop secure and detailed knowledge across the personal, social, health and economic education, relationships and (where relevant) sex education programme and citizenship curriculum.
suggested_evidence: Leaders ensure that the PSHE, RSHE, and citizenship curriculum is coherently planned, expertly taught, and regularly reviewed for quality and impact, fully aligned with the 2026 DfE statutory guidance.
Evidence includes curriculum maps, lesson observations, assessment records, and pupil voice confirming that pupils develop secure, detailed knowledge and can apply it in real-life contexts.

Leaders ensure that all staff teaching PSHE/RSHE have secure subject knowledge and receive ongoing, high-quality professional development, especially where non-specialists are involved. Monitoring records show that teaching is consistently effective, and leaders systematically review and develop staff expertise to ensure that non-specialist teaching does not limit the quality or impact of personal development provision
Leaders monitor the impact of the programme through regular reviews, triangulating feedback from pupils, staff, and parents, and adapting provision to address emerging needs
For strong standard, evidence must show depth, precision, and sustained impact, with pupils demonstrating secure knowledge and understanding across all strands, not just coverage or compliance.

judgement_areas.statement: Pupils are confident, resilient and independent. They are reflective, behave with integrity and cooperate consistently well with others. They are very well prepared for life beyond school.
suggested_evidence: Pupil voice, lesson observations, and enrichment participation confirm that pupils are confident, resilient, independent, and reflective.
Evidence shows pupils behave with integrity, cooperate well with others, and are well prepared for life beyond school.
Leaders monitor and review the impact of personal development activities, including leadership opportunities, mentoring, and peer support, ensuring that all pupils benefit.
Staff training and pupil programmes are aligned with statutory guidance and best practice

For strong standard, evidence must show that these qualities are consistently developed and demonstrated by all pupils, with clear impact on their readiness for life beyond school, not just participation or general improvement

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders and staff have a deep understanding of all their pupils and are systematic in anticipating and identifying which individuals or groups might need additional pastoral support. What is provided is highly effective.
suggested_evidence: Leaders and staff use a range of data, pupil voice, and pastoral records to systematically identify and anticipate which pupils or groups may need additional support. Evidence includes detailed tracking of pastoral interventions, regular reviews of impact, and case studies demonstrating highly effective support for vulnerable pupils.
Leaders ensure that support is proactive, tailored, and sustained, with staff trained in statutory guidance and best practice
For strong standard, evidence must show systematic, proactive identification and highly effective, sustained support for all who need it, not just availability or reactive provision

judgement_areas.statement: Secondary-age pupils have a wide range of opportunities to learn about their options for education, support and training after school. The school engages well with employers in the local area to ensure that pupils are well informed. Pupils know what they need to do to achieve their ambitions.
suggested_evidence: Leaders ensure that secondary-age pupils have a wide range of opportunities to learn about future education, training, and employment, including meaningful encounters with a range of providers in line with the Baker Clause and statutory careers guidance.

Evidence includes a published provider access policy, records of employer engagement, careers events, and pupil feedback confirming that pupils are well informed and know how to achieve their ambitions.
Leaders monitor the quality and impact of careers education, ensuring full compliance with the Baker Clause and steady progress towards the Gatsby benchmarks

For strong standard, evidence must show a wide range of high-quality, meaningful opportunities, strong employer engagement, and that all pupils are well informed and ambitious, not just that provision is in place

judgement_areas.statement: The programme of personal development is extensive and carefully tailored to the school’scontext and pupils’ aspirations. Pupils, including disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care, and those who may face barriers to their learning and/or well-being, participate and benefit fully.
suggested_evidence: Leaders ensure the personal development programme is extensive, carefully tailored to the school’s context and pupils’ aspirations, and accessible to all. Evidence includes participation logs, records of adaptations, and reviews of impact for disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, and other vulnerable groups.
Leaders monitor participation and benefit, making proactive adjustments to ensure full inclusion and sustained impact.
Pupil voice and case studies confirm that all groups participate and benefit fully
For strong standard, evidence must show the programme is extensive, tailored, and that all groups participate and benefit fully, not just that opportunities exist or are tracked

judgement_areas.statement: Evidence of the impact of the personal development programme is clear on the outcomes and experiences of pupils, including the groups of pupils listed above.
suggested_evidence: Leaders systematically evaluate the impact of the personal development programme on outcomes and experiences for all pupils, including disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, and other vulnerable groups. Evidence includes impact reviews, pupil and parent surveys, case studies, and analysis of participation and outcomes.
Leaders use this evaluation to refine provision and ensure sustained, positive impact for all groups
For strong standard, evidence must show clear, sustained impact on outcomes and experiences for all groups, not just general improvement or compliance.

statements: A significant minority of pupils do not receive a wide, rich range of experiences.
Pupils do not receive the pastoral support they need.
Leaders and/or governors/trustees undermine or fail to promote equality and diversity.
Significant weaknesses in the quality of the school’s personal development programme are not identified or tackled.
Leaders have established a culture and/or environment that places pupils under undue pressure or undermines their emotional health and/or well-being.
The curriculum fails to teach pupils about healthy relationships in line with the statutory relationships and sex education guidance.
notes: Do any of these statements apply? If they do not, you should be looking at the needs attention criteria.

statements: Leaders have appropriate ambitions for pupils’ personal development and well-being but weaknesses or inconsistencies in practice have a negative impact on pupils in general or on a particular group.

Leaders have only recently started to take action to improve pupils’ personal development and well-being. While their actions are appropriate, they are at an early stage. This means it is too soon to determine the impact of this work.
Leaders have considered pupils’ needs when developing the personal development programme, but it is not well matched to, or does not keep up with, their evolving circumstances.
notes: These are examples; they do not form statements like the expected standard does. There is no suggested evidence here, as what is listed provides examples of why the expected standard might not be met. Instead, use the expected standard to determine whether the school meets the required criteria. If a school does not meet one or more expected standard statements, it is considered to need attention (NA) in that area, unless an Urgent Improvement statement fully applies, in which case the school is graded as Urgent Improvement (UI). Needs Attention is not a grade criterion, but a guide to help identify areas where practice falls short of the expected standard and to support targeted improvement.

statements: Exceptionally high standards of personal development and well-being have been sustained. Pupils feel well supported, develop consistently detailed knowledge and skills, make a strong positive contribution to the school’s inclusive culture and are exceptionally well prepared for their next steps and for life beyond school.

Leaders’ actions have a transformational impact on the personal development and well-being of disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care, and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being. These pupils are now able to thrive in school and beyond the school.
There are no significant areas for improvement that leaders have not already prioritised
notes: Are leaders able to clearly articulate what is actually transformational or exceptional about what the school does? What is truly special about what the school does in terms of how they might have gone to exceptional lengths to overcome barriers or provide a specialist provision that is truly transformational in terms of those pupils life chances? The school will probably already have done work to model the provision as best practice in the region, or be at the stage where they could.

Expected Standard.focus: Expected standard focuses on systems being in place, statutory compliance, and regular review

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders identify pupils’ needs quickly and accurately, including any emerging or changing needs. This includes the needs of disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care, and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being.
key_questions:
Are there effective systems in place to identify pupils’ needs, including emerging needs, and do these align with the profile of need in the IDSR?
How are staff and parents able to raise concerns, and how is parent voice incorporated?
How does the pupil premium strategy identify key barriers, and how are safeguarding vulnerabilities and overlaps addressed?
How are needs assessed, including the role of the SENCO and external professionals?
suggested_evidence: The school has a documented system for identifying emerging or changing needs, and staff follow it.
Staff record concerns about pupils’ needs using agreed procedures.
Leaders use assessment information (historic, current and baseline assessments) to identify pupils with barriers to learning or well-being quickly, including between phases and at key transition

Leaders know their data and context: how achievement/attainment compares to national (use of IDSR); why these interventions and not others; where strong, inclusive T&L is in the school and where it is weaker; strength of TAs; pastoral issues
Leaders gather information from parents/carers as part of the identification process.
Evidence of needs identification is recorded in school systems (e.g., provision maps, CPOMS notes, SEND registers).
sources: Provision maps, SEND registers and safeguarding systems (e.g. CPOMS) showing how concerns are logged and followed up; IDSR and internal data used alongside leaders’ contextual knowledge to understand patterns of need; records of concerns raised by staff and parents and how these are considered; assessment records including baseline, transition and specialist assessments; SENCO records showing assessment activity and involvement of external professionals where appropriate; pupil premium strategy documentation identifying barriers faced by disadvantaged pupils; safeguarding records identifying vulnerability and overlap between need types.
For expected standard, evidence should demonstrate that systems for identification and assessment are in place and meet statutory expectations

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders have high expectations for these pupils. Typically, the support they provide (following specialist advice if needed) reduces barriers to their learning and/or well-being.
key_questions: Is support well targeted to support pupils to be able to access the curriculum? How effectively do teachers and TA’s support pupils need through quality first teaching in the classroom? How suitably are interventions targeted to support need? How precisely is this matched to need?
suggested_evidence: Leaders publish and review expectations for all pupil groups, including disadvantaged pupils, pupils with SEND, and pupils known to SC.
Reasonable adjustments made for pupils with SEND are documented, communicated to staff, and visible in lesson planning or classroom practice.
Leaders provide (and log) support/interventions aimed at addressing identified barriers.
Adaptations to curriculum, environment or routines are recorded and shared with relevant staff.
The accessibility plan meets statutory requirements, is reviewed within the required timeframe, and actions are documented.
Monitoring shows universal inclusive practice/ordinarily available provision strategies are used in classrooms so that students with SEND are supported equally well across all subects/classrooms
Monitoring shows that strategies on individual support plans are known and implemented across classrooms
Records show that adjustments for disadvantaged pupils are put in place and reviewed at set intervals.
sources: Published expectations for disadvantaged pupils and pupils with SEND; lesson planning and curriculum documentation showing reasonable adjustments; intervention timetables and logs; accessibility plan and review records; monitoring records of inclusive classroom practice; staff training records linked to SEND and inclusion; communications with parents regarding support provided.
For expected standard, evidence should show that support is planned, documented and reviewed.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders take a graduated approach (as explained earlier), which means pupils’ needs are generally met. Staff receive suitable training and support to implement this approach.
key_questions: Are teachers aware of pupils needs and equipped to meet them both in and beyond the classroom? Have staff received appropriate training to deliver support effectively?
suggested_evidence: The school has a written graduated approach process (assess–plan–do–review), and staff follow the documented steps.
Pupils have quality assured SMART termly targets which are co-produced with families and students for continual review
Pupil support plans include evidence of assessment, planned actions, implementation, and review dates.
Records show that external specialists are contacted when criteria are met and their recommendations are added to pupil plans.
Leaders schedule and log staff training linked to the graduated approach.
Notes from review meetings are kept and include decisions about adapting support.
Transition information for pupils with SEND is recorded and transferred to relevant staff or settings.
Communications with parents and professionals are logged.
sources: Written graduated approach documentation (assess–plan–do–review); individual pupil support plans with SMART targets; review meeting notes showing adaptation of provision; logs of external specialist advice and how it informs plans; staff training records linked to graduated response; transition records for pupils with SEND.
For expected standard, evidence should show the graduated approach is understood and followed

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders have a secure understanding of these pupils’ needs and the progress they make. They use appropriate evidence to inform their pupil premium strategy, including when selecting approaches to take. The strategy and approaches are generally understood and implemented by staff.
key_questions: How do leaders evaluate the impact of support adn interventions, including the PP strategy? Is the PP strategy evidence based, well understood by staff, and matched to the school's needs? Is there evidence of how the school has supported PP to overcome barriers?
suggested_evidence: Leaders maintain a current overview of disadvantaged pupils’ needs (e.g., pupil profiles, data summaries).
Education, health and care plans are up to date, with emergency reviews where needs change
Insidivudal support/learning plans include precise identification of need and identify required reasonable adjustments
Pupil support plans include identification of need and for children with EHCP to align with outcomes in section E and provision in section F
The pupil premium strategy includes links to research sources and identifies how actions relate to specific barriers.
Pupil Premium spend has a positive impact on pupils’ learning
Staff are aware of their role and responsibility in outcomes within the pupil premium strategy.
Monitoring records show whether planned PP actions are impleemented, evaluated and reviewed.

Leaders track the termly progress of pupils with SEND (including SEN K) using agreed assessment points and there is evidence of how these are used to further refine classroom practice and intervention.
Leaders ensure review processes are evaluative and measurable by embedding SMART termly targets and strengthening pupil voice so progress informs next steps
Updates to SEND provision are documented following progress reviews.
Updates to PP strategy are documented following progress reviews.
The SEN Information Report is published and updated annually as required.
sources: Pupil profiles and data summaries for disadvantaged pupils and pupils with SEND; EHCP documentation aligned to outcomes and provision; pupil premium strategy with links to research and identified barriers; monitoring records showing implementation and review of PP actions; staff briefings outlining responsibilities within the PP strategy; SEND Information Report published and updated annually.
For expected standard, evidence should show leaders understand needs and review impact.

judgement_areas.statement: The qualified SENCo has sufficient authority within the leadership structure to make a positive difference for pupils with SEND.
key_questions: Does the SENCO have whole school responsibility? Are all of SLT working with, alongside and supporting the SENCO so that they're not operating in isolation?
suggested_evidence: The SENCo is listed as part of the school’s leadership structure.
The SENCo has designated time to carry out leadership duties relating to SEND.
The SENCo attends leadership meetings
The SENCo leads whole-school SEND processes (e.g., training, policy updates, monitoring), with records kept.
sources: School staffing structure showing the SENCO’s position; records of SENCO attendance at leadership meetings; timetabled leadership time for SEND; SENCO-led training and policy updates; monitoring records led by the SENCO; evidence of collaboration between SENCO and SLT.
For expected standard, evidence should show the SENCO has authority and support

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders are committed to, and understand, their role in the local area partnership’s strategy to improve the experiences of, and outcomes for, pupils with SEND. Where appropriate, they ensure that local partnership strategies have a positive impact on pupils at the school.
key_questions: Are challenges in working with organisations, such as LA, been identified and addressed and how are gaps in local partnership support mitigated?
suggested_evidence: The school participates in local area SEND meetings or networks, with attendance recorded.
Leaders share information about the local offer with parents through published materials or direct communication.
Leaders make timely referrals for support to outside agencies and professionals as part of early-identification and interact with the LA as part of the graduated response
Leaders complete required documentation for LA statutory assessments (e.g., EHCP requests, updates) within expected timescales.
sources: Records of participation in local area SEND meetings or networks; documentation showing information shared with parents about the local offer; logs of timely referrals to external agencies; completed LA statutory assessment paperwork; records of how local partnership strategies influence school provision.
For expected standard, evidence should show statutory engagement and cooperation

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders support pupils who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care, including looked-after and previously looked after children, well. Staff work effectively, including with the virtual school, so that pupils’ personal education plans generally improve their learning opportunities and experiences.
key_questions: Is there effective collaboration with the virtual school and other agencies, as evidenced in safeguarding records?
suggested_evidence: The designated teacher for LAC/PLAC holds the required qualification/experience (noted in the school’s staffing structure) and staff are aware of who this is.
Each looked-after child has a Personal Education Plan uploaded on the expected schedule.
Leaders monitor the implementation of support identified on the PEP
Leaders use social care information to inform decisions about attendance, support, or well-being in school systems.
The school logs communications with social workers, virtual school heads, and other professionals.
Information required by the local authority is shared through documented channels.
sources: Records identifying the designated teacher for looked-after and previously looked-after children; Personal Education Plans completed and reviewed within expected timescales; safeguarding records evidencing multi-agency involvement; logs of communication with social workers and virtual school heads; use of social care information to inform support in school.
For expected standard, evidence should show appropriate support and compliance

judgement_areas.statement: Alternative provision is commissioned appropriately and is used in pupils’ best interests. Leaders take responsibility for the education and welfare of pupils who are placed in it.
key_questions: Are there robust processes for checking the quality and suitability of AP, with decisions and ongoing reviews clearly documented? is there careful analysis and racking for pupils accessing significant amoutns of online tutoring? Can you articulate what a pupil's week looks like (who accesses AP)? Can you articulate how the decisions for them to access AP are in their best interests?
suggested_evidence: Leaders carry out and record suitability and safeguarding checks before placing a pupil in alternative provision.

Decisions to refer pupils to AP are made with due thoughtfulness and recorded with clear reasons (e.g. Case studies where leaders have revisited AP decisions to ensure placements are in pupils’ best interests)
Leaders monitor attendance, curriculum, and progress for pupils in AP and keep written records.
Leaders maintain responsibility for safeguarding checks and receive regular updates from the AP provider.
sources: Suitability and safeguarding checks for alternative provision providers; records of decision-making for AP placements showing pupils’ best interests; monitoring records of attendance, curriculum and progress for pupils in AP; logs of regular communication with AP providers; tracking and analysis for pupils receiving significant online tutoring.
For expected standard, evidence should show appropriate commissioning and oversight

Strong Standard.focus: To meet the strong standard, schools must show consistency, not pockets of good practice; precision and not general monitoring; sustained impact and not short-term gains; experience and opportunity, not just attainment.

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders and staff establish strategies that consistently enhance the opportunities and experiences of disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care, and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being.
key_questions: Can the school demonstrate that inclusion strategies "consistently enhance" opporrtunities and experiences for all vulnerable groups, with systems in place to track and evaluate this across all areas?
suggested_evidence: The school has clearly defined inclusion strategies, that go beyond compliance, and staff implement them consistently across all areas of provision.
Leadership of inclusion is strong: the approach is proactive, joined up and deliberately preventative.
A strong culture of belonging is evident across the school
Training for TAs who support children with SEND is contextually appropriate and enhances the learning and experience for pupils.

Staff apply agreed inclusion strategies to enhance pupils' experiences and not just support learning e.g. pupils can access enrichment opportunities, leadership roles, trips, and the wider curriculum.
Teachers routinely use inclusive teaching practices as part of standard classroom provision, rather than relying on individual or additional interventions.
Staff demonstrate a shared understanding of the barriers faced by different pupil groups and follow agreed approaches to address these barriers.
Wider transition programmes (enhanced programmes) are in place and are used with positive impact on transition for vulnerable pupils
Curriculum adaptations are made in line with school guidance to maintain curriculum ambition and avoid narrowing pupils’ learning experiences.
The school records evidence of improved participation, confidence, belonging and engagement through case studies and other monitoring systems.
The school analyses participation data to identify and address barriers that reduce attendance

For strong standard, evidence must show that inclusion strategies are implemented consistently across all areas of provision, leading to sustained improvements in opportunities and experiences for all vulnerable groups, not just isolated examples or compliance
sources: Inclusion strategy documentation embedded across the school, monitoring records evidencing consistent application, participation and enrichment data, case studies showing sustained improvement in belonging and engagement, curriculum adaptation records maintaining ambition, staff and pupil voice analysis, ‘you said, we did’ records showing systematic influence on decisions

For strong standard, evidence must show consistency across all areas, precision in implementation and sustained impact on pupils’ opportunities and experiences, rather than isolated examples or the existence of strategies

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders and staff rigorously monitor the progress of these pupils and consistently ensure that any barriers to success are swiftly and effectively addressed. Strategies are systematically and skilfully adjusted as needed, so that they make a sustained difference to pupils’ opportunities and experiences. Well-analysed, quantitative and qualitative data underpins leaders’ decisions.
key_questions: Can the school demonstrate rigorous montoring and systematic action to address barriers, with precise analysis and targeted decisions leading to sustained improvement?
suggested_evidence: The school gathers regular feedback from pupils on how support strategies help them access learning and feel included, and this information is recorded, reviewed and acted upon.
The school operates systematic, frequent and purposefully planned review cycles for vulnerable pupils, with outcomes recorded and monitored through established systems.
Leaders can clearly articulate what the data indicates, the actions taken in response, and the rationale for selecting those actions, in line with school procedures
Leaders analyse and act on quantitative data (including progress, attendance and behaviour) alongside agreed qualitative evidence sources such as pupil voice, parental feedback and staff insight
The school maintains evidence that identified barriers are addressed promptly, in accordance with monitoring expectations, and are not allowed to persist unchecked.
Staff adjust strategies in response to review findings, refining existing approaches rather than replacing them without evidence of need
Case studies document the identification of barriers, the refinement of interventions, and the sustained improvement demonstrated over time
Monitoring records show consistent and sustained improvements in pupils’ opportunities and wider school experiences as a result of implemented actions.
Literacy interventions reliably diagnose decoding, flunecy or comprehension needs and enables teachers to reflect these insights in planning and classroom practice.
Evidence-based strategies are used to raise outcomes and the effectiveness of these strategies and intervnetions are regularly reviewed with measurable data

For strong standard, evidence must demonstrate rigorous, systematic monitoring and precise adjustment of strategies, resulting in sustained impact and measurable improvements for vulnerable pupils, rather than general monitoring or short-term gains.
sources: Planned review cycle records, quantitative and qualitative data analysis, pupil and parent feedback, monitoring logs showing adjustments made in response to evidence, case studies demonstrating refinement of strategies and sustained improvement, attendance, behaviour and participation analysis
For strong standard, evidence must demonstrate rigorous, frequent monitoring and precise adjustment of strategies leading to sustained impact, not general monitoring or short-term improvement

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders ensure that the pupil premium strategy is implemented and monitored effectively, including through ongoing, high-quality training and support for staff.
key_questions: Is the lived experience of disadvantged pupils reflected in pupil voice and practice, with clear evidence of impact?
suggested_evidence: The pupil premium strategy is fully integrated into wider school improvement
Staff at all levels are able to explain the key barriers faced by disadvantaged pupils and outline how their everyday practice addresses these barriers.
The school provides high‑quality continuing professional development (CPD) focused on inclusive teaching and supporting disadvantaged pupils
Leaders ensure that all CPD is followed up through observation, coaching or review to confirm that practice has changed as intended
Leaders monitor both the fidelity of implementation and the impact of strategies over time, using established evaluation processes
Adaptations to the pupil premium strategy are made in a timely manner and are informed by evidence gathered through ongoing monitoring
Monitoring shows clear evidence that disadvantaged pupils consistently access high‑quality teaching and benefit from effective support across subjects and phases

For strong standard, evidence must show that the pupil premium strategy is fully integrated into school improvement, with staff at all levels able to articulate and evidence how their practice addresses key barriers, and leaders demonstrating sustained impact through ongoing evaluation and adaptation
sources: Pupil premium strategy fully integrated into school improvement documentation, staff training and CPD records focused on inclusive practice, observation, coaching and review records confirming changes in practice, evaluation records showing fidelity and impact over time, monitoring showing consistent access to high-quality teaching, ‘you said, we did’ records evidencing sustained influence on practice

For strong standard, evidence must show systematic integration, staff articulation of impact and sustained improvement over time; having a pupil premium strategy document alone is expected but not sufficient to demonstrate strong practice

statements: Leaders do not identify and assess pupils’ needs effectively. This has a significant negative impact on how well the school supports pupils’ learning and/or well-being.

Support for disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being is ineffective.

Leaders do not meet statutory requirements for pupils who are disadvantaged and/or those with SEND and/or those who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being. This has a significant negative impact on pupils’ learning and/or well-being.
notes: Do any of these statements apply? If they do not, you should be looking at the needs attention criteria.

statements: Leaders have only recently started to take appropriate action to identify and assess pupils’ needs or reduce barriers to pupils’ learning and/or well-being.
Weaknesses or inconsistencies in practice have a negative impact on a particular group of pupils.
Leaders’ use of alternative provision has limited positive impact on pupils’ learning and/or well-being.
notes: These are examples; they do not form statements like the expected standard does. There is no suggested evidence here, as what is listed provides examples of why the expected standard might not be met. Instead, use the expected standard to determine whether the school meets the required criteria. If a school does not meet one or more expected standard statements, it is considered to need attention (NA) in that area, unless an Urgent Improvement statement fully applies, in which case the school is graded as Urgent Improvement (UI). Needs Attention is not a grade criterion, but a guide to help identify areas where practice falls short of the expected standard and to support targeted improvement.

statements: Exceptional standards of inclusion have been sustained over time so that barriers to learning and/or well-being for pupils, including disadvantaged pupils, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care, and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being, are reduced exceptionally well to ensure highly positive outcomes and experiences for pupils.
Leaders’ actions have a transformational impact on how well these pupils achieve and thrive across all areas of school life, and have ensured that they feel they belong within the school community.
There are no significant areas for improvement that leaders have not already prioritised.
notes: Are leaders able to clearly articulate what is actually transformational or exceptional about what the school does? What is truly special about what the school does in terms of how they might have gone to exceptional lengths to overcome barriers or provide a specialist provision that is truly transformational in terms of those pupils life chances? The school will probably already have done work to model the provision as best practice in the region, or be at the stage where they could.

Expected Standard.focus: Expected standard focuses on systems being in place, statutory compliance, and regular review

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders prioritise the early years to give children a successful start to their education and the best chance of later success. They have an accurate understanding of the quality and impact of education and care in this phase, and an effective strategy to bring about improvements.
key_questions:
How clearly can leaders articulate the intent of the EYFS curriculum across the 7 areas of learning?
• How does the curriculum build children’s knowledge and skills progressively over time?
• How do leaders know what children are learning and how well the curriculum is working in practice?
• How does assessment support leaders in understanding the impact of provision and identifying areas for improvement?
suggested_evidence: Leaders demonstrate a clear commitment to prioritising early years, evidenced by strategic plans, resource allocation, and regular review of provision.
They use a range of information—including staff feedback, parent voice, and child outcomes—to maintain an accurate understanding of quality and impact.
Leaders monitor progress against improvement plans and act promptly to address any weaknesses

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders know the statutory requirements of the EYFS and make sure they are met. They have a clear vision for providing high-quality education and care for children in the early years and the same high expectations of them as they do for pupils in the rest of the school.
key_questions:
• How do leaders ensure that EYFS statutory requirements are met consistently in practice?
• What is leaders’ vision for early years, and how is this reflected in curriculum design and daily practice?
• How do leaders ensure that expectations for children in early years are ambitious and aligned with the rest of the school?
• How do leaders assure themselves that provision is not dependent on unstructured activity without clear learning intent?wledge and skills.
suggested_evidence: Leaders ensure all statutory requirements of the EYFS are met, including safeguarding, welfare, learning and development, and assessment. Evidence includes compliance audits, policy reviews, and staff training records.
Leaders articulate a clear vision for high-quality education and care, with expectations for early years matching those for the rest of the school.
Staff are regularly updated on statutory changes and best practice

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders and staff make sure that education and care practices are suitable for the age and stage of children’s development.
key_questions:
How do leaders and staff ensure that provision reflects children’s developmental stages and needs?
• How does the learning environment support purposeful, high-quality learning opportunities?
• How effectively do adults interact with children to develop communication, language and thinking?
suggested_evidence: Leaders and staff plan and deliver education and care practices that are developmentally appropriate, drawing on assessment information, child observations, and parent feedback. Evidence includes curriculum plans, lesson observations, and records of adaptations for individual needs.
Staff receive ongoing training in child development and the EYFS framework, ensuring practices are responsive to children’s age and stage

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders make sure that the curriculum identifies and sequences the key knowledge that children will learn across the EYFS educational programmes.
key_questions:
What evidence is there that the EYFS curriculum identifies the key knowledge children are expected to learn?
• How is learning sequenced so that children build on prior knowledge and experience?
• How do leaders assure themselves that curriculum sequencing is implemented consistently?
suggested_evidence: Leaders ensure the curriculum is coherently planned and sequenced, identifying key knowledge and skills across all seven areas of learning.
Staff understand curriculum intent and sequence, and adapt teaching to ensure children build on prior learning

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders ensure that the curriculum is well taught and that staff engage in high-quality interactions with children.
key_questions:
How do leaders monitor and assure the quality of teaching in the early years?
• How do they ensure that all staff, including teaching assistants, understand and deliver high-quality interactions?
• What professional development is in place to improve practice?
suggested_evidence: Leaders monitor teaching quality and ensure staff engage in high-quality interactions, including modelling, questioning, and scaffolding learning. Evidence includes lesson observations, staff training records, and feedback from children and parents.
Leaders provide targeted support and professional development to improve teaching and interactions

judgement_areas.statement: Staff consider children’s starting points in their curriculum design and approach to teaching so that gaps in children’s knowledge are identified and tackled.
key_questions:
How are children’s starting points identified and used to inform curriculum and teaching?
• How do staff identify gaps in learning and respond to them in practice?
• How do leaders know whether gaps are being addressed effectively over time?
suggested_evidence: Staff use assessment information and observations to identify children’s starting points and plan curriculum and teaching accordingly.
Staff monitor gaps in knowledge and skills, providing targeted support and reviewing impact

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders make sure that early years teachers inform Year 1 teachers about any gaps in children’s knowledge to ensure an effective transition to key stage 1.
key_questions:
How do leaders ensure that information about children’s learning is shared effectively with Year 1 teachers?
• How well do staff across EYFS and KS1 understand what has been taught and what comes next?
• How is continuity of learning secured through transition?
suggested_evidence: Leaders ensure robust transition arrangements, including sharing assessment information and profiles with Year 1 teachers.
Leaders monitor the effectiveness of transition and address any gaps in knowledge or skills

judgement_areas.statement: There is a sharp focus on making sure that children acquire a wide vocabulary, communicate effectively and, in Reception, secure their knowledge of phonics.
key_questions:
How is the development of vocabulary and communication prioritised across the provision?
• How do leaders ensure that phonics teaching in Reception is systematic and effective?
• How do they monitor the impact of this provision on children’s early reading development?
suggested_evidence: Leaders and staff prioritise vocabulary development, communication, and phonics, using language-rich environments, modelling, and targeted interventions.
Staff receive training in language development and phonics teaching

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders ensure that staff provide effectively for children’s personal, social and emotional development, including making sure that they feel safe, secure, stimulated and happy.
key_questions:
How do leaders ensure that provision supports children’s personal, social and emotional development?
• How do staff create an environment in which children feel safe, secure and ready to learn?
• How do leaders know whether this aspect of provision is effective?
suggested_evidence: Leaders and staff provide a nurturing environment, prioritising personal, social, and emotional development.
Staff receive training in PSED and safeguarding, and provision is regularly reviewed for quality and impact

judgement_areas.statement: Children are being well prepared to reach a good level of development by the end of Reception. Typically, this will be reflected in the proportion of children reaching a good level of development being broadly in line with national averages.
key_questions:
How do leaders track children’s progress towards achieving a good level of development?
• How do outcomes compare with national benchmarks or contextual expectations?
• How do leaders respond where children start from lower starting points?
suggested_evidence: Leaders monitor progress towards a good level of development, using assessment data, tracking, and intervention records. Evidence includes EYFS Profile outcomes, progress reviews, and records of targeted support for children at risk of falling behind
Leaders review outcomes against national averages and act to address any gaps

judgement_areas.statement: By the end of Reception, children use their knowledge of phonics to read accurately and with increasing fluency.
key_questions:
How do leaders know that children can apply their phonics knowledge in reading by the end of Reception?
• How does early reading provision prepare children for Year 1 expectations?
Can we see this in terms of how well pupils can read?
suggested_evidence: Leaders and staff monitor phonics progress, using tracking, lesson observations, and assessment records.
Staff receive training in phonics teaching and adapt provision to ensure all children achieve fluency

judgement_areas.statement: Children develop appropriate knowledge and skills across the 7 areas of learning, relevant to their age and stage of development. Children, including disadvantaged children, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being typically achieve well from their starting points. This means that they are generally ready for the next stage of learning.
key_questions:
How do leaders assure themselves that children develop appropriate knowledge and skills across all areas of learning?
• How well do different groups (including disadvantaged children, children with SEND and other vulnerable groups) achieve from their starting points?
• How do leaders identify and address barriers to learning for these groups in practice and prioritise identified barriers e.g low communication skills or needing support with toileting etc.
suggested_evidence: Leaders and staff monitor progress across all seven areas of learning
Evidence includes tracking for disadvantaged children, those with SEND, and vulnerable groups, with records of adaptations and targeted support.
Leaders review outcomes to ensure all children are ready for the next stage of learning

judgement_areas.statement: Relevant leaders, including the headteacher, are highly knowledgeable about the early years. They make astute decisions about how the curriculum and teaching should adapt and evolve, based on their evidence and insight about how well children have learned what was intended.
key_questions: Can the school show consistency, not pockets of good practice; precision and not general monitoring; sustained impact and not short-term gains?
suggested_evidence: Leaders, including the headteacher, demonstrate deep knowledge of early years pedagogy, statutory requirements, and best practice.
They use detailed assessment information, lesson observations, and feedback from staff and parents to make astute decisions about curriculum and teaching adaptations.
Leaders’ insight is used to refine curriculum content and teaching approaches, ensuring learning is embedded and responsive to children’s needs
For strong standard, evidence must show leaders’ knowledge is precise, decisions are insight-driven, and adaptations lead to sustained, embedded improvement across the phase

judgement_areas.statement: Leaders are highly effective in quality assuring the impact of education and care in the early years, which allows them to make rapid and sustained improvements when necessary.
suggested_evidence: Leaders implement robust quality assurance processes, including regular monitoring, feedback, and review of education and care.

Evidence shows rapid and sustained improvements in response to identified needs, using feedback and review processes to inform quality assurance, ensuring provision is consistently high quality and improvements are embedded
For strong standard, evidence must show quality assurance is highly effective, rapid, and leads to sustained improvement, not just periodic or reactive action

judgement_areas.statement: Staff consistently maximise the opportunities for engaging children in high-quality interactions throughout the day, including during informal times and through care routines. They proactively seek out children who engage less readily with staff or other children.
suggested_evidence: Staff consistently engage children in high-quality interactions, including during informal times and care routines.
Staff receive ongoing training in interaction strategies and child development, ensuring all children benefit from rich, responsive engagement
For strong standard, evidence must show consistency, proactive engagement of all children, and sustained impact on learning and development, not just general practice or occasional support

judgement_areas.statement: Typically, children’s achievement will be reflected in above-average proportions reaching a good level of development.
suggested_evidence: Leaders monitor achievement using assessment data, tracking, and intervention records, with evidence showing above-average proportions of children reaching a good level of development.
Leaders review outcomes against national benchmarks and act to address any gaps, ensuring sustained improvement.
For strong standard, evidence must show sustained, above-average achievement and rapid improvement for all groups, not just general progress or isolated gains

judgement_areas.statement: Children, including disadvantaged children, those with SEND, those who are known (or previously known) to children’s social care, and those who may face other barriers to their learning and/or well-being, develop a broad vocabulary and a detailed understanding across the 7 areas of learning and development. They are exceptionally well prepared for the next stage of learning.
suggested_evidence: Leaders and staff monitor progress across all seven areas of learning, using assessment information, intervention records, and feedback from children and parents.
Evidence shows disadvantaged children, those with SEND, and vulnerable groups develop broad vocabulary and detailed understanding, with records of adaptations and targeted support.
Leaders review outcomes to ensure all children are exceptionally well prepared for the next stage of learning, with sustained impact and full inclusion

For strong standard, evidence must show all groups develop detailed knowledge and skills, are exceptionally well prepared, and benefit from sustained, high-quality provision, not just general readiness or participation

statements: Leaders have not met the statutory requirements of the EYFS in full. This has a significant negative impact on children’s safety and well-being and/or learning and development.
Leaders have low expectations of what children in the early years can and should achieve.
The curriculum is poorly designed. It does not meet children’s needs or provide the necessary foundations for future learning.

judgement_areas.statement: Generally, students have age-appropriate knowledge and skills across the curriculum. This is reflected in the quality of their responses and the work they produce.

judgement_areas.statement: Students are generally ready for the next stage of education, employment or training. They go on to destinations that meet their interests, career goals and aspirations.

shared_guidance: Purpose:

This tool is designed to support senior leaders in evaluating and evidencing the quality of leadership and governance across key areas of school provision. It aligns with Ofsted and Trust expectations, promoting consistency, rigour, and reflective practice.
How to Use:
1. Read the judgement area
• To meet this standard you have to be able to demonstrate that you meet each bullet point. Review each bullet point to understand what is required for the standard.
2. Start with Key Questions:
• Use the key questions to frame your evaluation, considering how an inspector might interpret each area.
3. Gather and Triangulate Evidence:
• Refer to the suggested evidence as a guide, not a checklist.
• Combine multiple sources (data, observations, feedback) to inform your judgement.
4. Apply the RAG Rating:
• Red: Insufficient evidence to meet the standard
• Amber: Partial evidence; you believe the standard is met but some evidence is insecure.
• Green: Strong, comprehensive evidence supports your judgement.
5. Commentary and Next Steps:
• For each area, write up to 150 words explaining your evidence and rationale.
• Write in a formal, objective style rather than first person.
• Focus on impact and learning (“what changed, what improved”), not just process.
• Draw on specific, triangulated evidence—refer to actual data, documents, and stakeholder feedback (e.g. IDSR, SEF, action plans, staff surveys, pupil outcomes).
• Where appropriate, clearly reference areas where provision has fallen short and describe the actions taken, including measurable outcomes or improvements.
• Avoid general statements; use precise examples that demonstrate knowledge of the school’s context and the effectiveness of actions
• Ensure your commentary enables others (Principal, peer SLT, Governors, Phase Director) to triangulate with other data sources and what is seen on the ground
• For next steps, identify gaps or areas for improvement based on your evaluation. Make these actionable and specific, referencing what will happen first and how progress will be monitored.

• If you have rated an area as green for expected standard, refer to the strong standard to highlight what would need to be in place for further improvement. For strong standard, reference exceptional (and so on)
• If you have rated an area as amber, focus next steps on addressing the gaps in evidence or practice needed to reach green for that standard.
6. Quality Assurance (QA)
• The Principal will review each area before final submission of the full OSED to ensure completion, judgement agreement and consistency in approach between leaders in the school.
• The Consultant Phase Director will provide termly QA moderation to ensure that leaders within and across schools are applying RAG ratings consistently
FAQ
What is strong evidence? | Multiple, reliable sources showing clear impact
How do I judge borderline cases? | Use triangulation and consult Principal
Who reviews my ratings? | Initially, the Principal. Then, during the QA process, the SI lead
What is a judgement area? | The specific aspect being evaluated within the area
What are the key questions? | Prompts to help leaders interpret what inspectors are looking for.
What is meant by suggested evidence? | Examples of what could be in place, or used to demonstrate meeting the standard.

What should I capture in my commentary? | Consider these prompt questions: “What specific evidence supports your judgement?”; “How does this evidence show impact or improvement?”; “Where has provision fallen short, and what actions were taken?”; “What are your next steps, and how will you monitor progress?”
